entry.title: Argument among Idaho elected officials breaks out over expenses at America250 meeting
entry.summary: NAMPA — A former Ridgevue High School teacher was arrested T… BOISE — Gear up for a weekend with dad and check out the 23r… Idaho Secretary of State Phil McGrane speaks to the America250 in Idaho Advisory Council on June 10, 2026, at the Idaho State Capitol in Boise. Originally published June 10 on IdahoCapitalSun.com. A late, unexpected request for additional money made by two of the warmup acts scheduled to perform at the upcoming America250 in Idaho Capitol Celebration in Boise played a role in setting off a contentious public argument between two statewide elected officials Wednesday at the Idaho State Capitol in Boise. For more than a year and a half, members of the America250 in Idaho Advisory Council have been planning local celebrations that are scheduled for July 4 to celebrate the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence being adopted in Philadelphia in 1776. With less than a month to go until the big events, the advisory council held one of its final meetings Wednesday to plan, coordinate and organize the America250 in Idaho celebrations, which are taking place in Boise and in local communities across the state. During Wednesday’s meeting, Idaho Secretary of State Phil McGrane, who is a member of the advisory council, made a motion to approve spending an additional $10,000 for unexpected expenses, including an additional $3,000 specifically requested by musicians Chad Marvin and EllieMae for the Boise celebration. McGrane also said there were additional unexpected budget considerations as well, including $1,600 for RV rentals to be used as “green rooms” for the performers and their equipment, $1,500 for Ada County Paramedics to staff the event and $6,000 to provide free water for the public. McGrane said the council has the funding available and the expenses were unexpected in nature, but came along with planning a large-scale event that he said could attract more than 20,000 people to the grounds surrounding the Idaho State Capitol. “We’re now at that point in it where we’re running up against the things, the unforeseen, and the issues that pop up (with planning large public events),” McGrane said. But Idaho State Treasurer Julie Ellsworth, who is also a member of the advisory council, said she has been “getting a lot of heat” for the amount of money the America250 in Idaho Advisory Council has been spending and balked at McGrane’s request. Ellsworth then publicly questioned McGrane’s motivations for the money. “There have been expenses that have been caused that have seen no fruition, that I believe have not really brought forth anything for this America250 in Idaho (celebration) in contracts and expenses – a lot of them you’ve been involved in, and ironically they equal about $10,000. And I’m just wondering if this is just to offset some of the work that was never approved but done, and somebody demanded money for?” Ellsworth asked McGrane during the meeting. McGrane responded immediately, and then offered a line-item breakdown of where the $10,000 would go toward, noting that any unused funding would be returned to the committee. “First of all, I find it offensive that you would suggest that,” McGrane told Ellsworth. “I think all of the work that was done was specifically for your office, and so … I’m not willing to engage unless you have anything specific that you want to infer or imply.” “I think there’s been extensive funding for some of your programs, and I don’t see this as a healthy debate,” McGrane added. “My focus, and the team in our office’s focus, is to make sure Idaho gets to celebrate America 250. This funding is specific to this, and I think the inference that there’s a blank check is offensive.” State officials said the advisory council has $54,967.53 in available funds to work with – enough to cover the $10,000. Marvin co-wrote and recorded “Esto Perpetua” an America250 in Idaho song that was already financed by the America250 in Idaho Advisory Council – at Ellsworth’s recommendation. Marvin and EllieMae have been announced as artists who will perform before headliner Josh Ritter during the America250 in Idaho Capitol Celebration, which is scheduled to take place July 4 at Cecil D. Andrus Park in front of the Idaho State Capitol. In addition to Ritter, other performers scheduled to perform at the America250 in Idaho Capitol Celebration include the Afrosonics, as well as Jeff Crosby and Willy Braun, according to members of the advisory council. However, members of the advisory council said Wednesday that while the announced performers have agreed to perform July 4, contracts with the musicians have not yet been signed. Committee members said that the local company Duck Club Entertainment, which created the annual Treefort Music Festival in Boise, is finalizing the contracts with the performers.

entry.title: IDOC outlines firing squad procedures as new policy nears implementation
entry.summary: The Idaho Maximum Security Institution in Kuna. BOISE — Idaho passed legislation last year making death by firing squad the primary means of execution in the state. As the implementation date nears, policy documents released by the Idaho Department of Correction this week outline how these changes will soon manifest in the state’s execution facility. In procedures outlining the time prior to the execution, IDOC documents state the inmate will receive their last meal served by 7 p.m. the day prior to the scheduled execution, and a mild sedative will be administered a few hours later, no later than 11 p.m. This will be followed by another sedative administered no later than four hours prior to the time of the execution. After this has been administered, the inmate will be moved to the execution chamber, where they will be restrained, have a target adhered to their chest over the heart and have the option to have an eye covering applied, according to IDOC documents. Procedures released Monday indicate the firing squad teams will consist of three team members, two alternates and a team leader, with a three-member team carrying out the execution. The members of the firing squad will be volunteers who are required to participate in a minimum of four training session per year and must: Documents state that .308 caliber rifles and .308 or 7.62 110 grain ammunition has been approved for firing squad execution. An IDOC spokesperson specified in an emailed statement that the rifles used will be the Daniel Defense DD5-P outfitted with a scope, suppressor and bipod. The total cost of each rifle is $4,843.94. If there are no legal impediments or a stay of execution, the firing squad members will load their weapons at the direction of the team leader. According to IDOC standard operating procedure (SOP), the team leader will load one live round in three separate magazines for each weapon, and each firing squad member will be issued issued a firearm and three magazines. The team leader will also provide the cadence for firing, which firing squad members will use to fire simultaneously. After the execution process is complete, the Ada County Coroner or deputy coroner will enter the execution chamber to pronounce the inmate as dead, followed by an announcement from the IDOC director that the death sentence has ben carried out. The changes arrive after Idaho Gov. Brad Little signed House Bill 37 into law in 2025, making firing squad the primary method of execution in the state and Idaho the first state in the nation to do so. Though signed more than a year ago, the bill was written to not go into effect until July 1 of this year. The Idaho Press previously reported the delay in implementation was made to allow for the Idaho Department of Correction to be provided the time needed to remodel its execution facility, as well as restructure its policy around firing squad executions. In June 2025, IDOC announced the retrofitting of its F-Block execution facility was underway to accommodate both lethal injection and firing squad executions. According to a prior release from IDOC, the changes included construction of a dedicated space for the firing squad and its associated procedures along with updating witness areas for observation. The total cost of these changes, which marked the second phase of upgrades, was estimated to be $910,802. The initial phase of the renovation, which included design and engineering, cost $313,915, according to an emailed statement from an IDOC spokesperson. Idaho’s initial shift toward allowing firing squads for election stemmed from a 2023 law designating firing squad as the state’s secondary method of execution to be used when lethal injection was unavailable. That law came after IDOC was unable to obtain the necessary drugs in 2022, resulting in the expiration of the death warrant for Gerald Pizzuto. Beyond difficulty in obtaining the drugs, repeated issues also arose in administering lethal injections. In 2024, the execution of Thomas Creech had to be called off after a medical team failed to insert an IV line eight times, as was reported by the Associated Press. Creech’s scheduled execution previously brought protest from local church and civil rights leaders, who voiced opposition to the death penalty. ACLU of Idaho, in the wake of Creech’s failed execution, called for Creech’s sentence to be commuted to life in prison, characterizing execution as a violation of the Eight Amendment’s protections against cruel and unusual punishment. IDOC currently has eight inmates under a death sentence. The last execution in the state was carried out in 2012. Royce McCandless is the Statehouse reporter and covers Idaho politics. You can email him at . Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. Sign up now!

entry.title: Body recovered from Payette River after Highway 55 crash
entry.summary: CASCADE — Authorities have recovered a body from the Payette River one week after a crash on Highway 55 sent a vehicle into the water. First responders recovered the body — which has not yet been publicly identified — from the river on Sunday afternoon, Idaho State Police announced. The body was located about three miles downstream of the crash site by a passerby, who notified law enforcement. The recovery effort was led by ISP, the Valley County Sheriff’s Office and Idaho Mountain Search and Rescue Unit. Investigators believe the individual was driving the Dodge Ram pickup that collided with another vehicle on Highway 55 south of Cascade on Sunday, May 31, and left the roadway before going into the North Fork of the Payette River. Evidence indicates the man was the sole occupant of the pickup “and that all individuals associated with the crash have been accounted for,” ISP said. The pickup was recovered from the river on June 1. The Valley County Coroner’s Office will release the identity of the deceased and determine the cause and manner of death as ISP continues to investigate the crash. Madison Guernsey is the News Editor at the Idaho Press. CASCADE — A pickup truck has been recovered from the Payette River, but its driver remains unaccounted for following a Sunday afternoon crash … Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. entry.title: US 9th Circuit judge faces battery charge after parking lot confrontation in Idaho Falls
entry.summary: Workers begin removing Trump''s name from Kennedy Center after judge ruled it was… President Donald Trump’s name remains on the facade of the Kennedy Center despit… U.S. 9th Circuit Court of Appeals Judge Ryan Douglas Nelson. IDAHO FALLS — U.S. 9th Circuit Court of Appeals Judge Ryan Douglas Nelson faces two criminal charges after police say he battered a man by swiping the glasses from his face, tossing them across an asphalt lot and stomping on them during an April confrontation over a parking space in Idaho Falls. Nelson, 52, of Idaho Falls, has been charged by Idaho Falls city prosecuting attorney Zachary H. Jones with battery and malicious injury to property, both misdemeanors, in Idaho’s 7th Judicial District Court, according to police and court records the Idaho State Journal recently obtained. The alleged incident occurred in a parking lot on Memorial Drive in Idaho Falls the afternoon of April 2. Nelson is alleged to have engaged in a verbal dispute with another Idaho Falls resident over a parking space that escalated into a physical altercation, court records show. Jones confirmed the charges filed against Nelson but would not comment further. “Given the potential high-profile nature of the case, I want to be cautious and make sure we have a clean process,” Jones said. When reached for comment Thursday, Nelson’s Idaho Falls attorney of record in the case, Curtis R. Smith of Smith Woolf Anderson & Wilkinson, said, “Mr. Nelson maintains a presumption of innocence. He will work through the judicial system to resolve these personal charges which allegedly resulted in a pair of broken sunglasses and are unrelated to his professional position.” Nelson was charged April 22 and entered not guilty pleas to both charges May 13, court records show. He is scheduled for a pretrial conference hearing in front of 7th District Magistrate Judge John C. Dewey at the Bonneville County Courthouse in Idaho Falls on June 18. The purpose of the pretrial conference hearing is to exchange evidence, negotiate plea agreements and manage the court schedule to either resolve the incident without trial or to prepare for trial. The U.S. 9th Circuit Court of Appeals is based in San Francisco with additional courthouses in Pasadena, California; Portland, Oregon; and Seattle. The appellate court’s jurisdiction includes Alaska, Arizona, California, Hawaii, Idaho, Montana, Nevada, Oregon and Washington. Nelson also has an office in his hometown of Idaho Falls. According to the alleged victim, whom the Idaho State Journal is not identifying because he’s reportedly the victim of a crime, the incident unfolded just after he had pulled into the parking space adjacent to Nelson’s blue pickup truck. The alleged victim says Nelson’s truck was angled into a parking space — the bulk of his truck in one spot with the right front tire and bumper hanging over the right line and into the spot to the right and the left rear part of the truck hanging over the left line and into the parking space to the left, effectively blocking three parking spots in the lot directly in front of the nearby businesses. As the alleged victim was exiting his white pickup truck, Nelson started his truck to leave the parking space. That’s when the alleged victim spoke. “I say ‘learn how to park,’” the alleged victim said. “I said it twice. That’s when he went crazy.” In a video of the incident obtained by the Idaho State Journal, the alleged victim walked around the front of Nelson’s truck and toward the entrance of a nearby business when Nelson got out of his truck and walked up to the alleged victim, talking to him and pointing. Nelson can be heard on the video saying he had been parked in the space for only two minutes. Within roughly 12 seconds of getting out of his truck, Nelson appears as if he swipes toward the face of the alleged victim in an exchange that ends with Nelson grabbing the glasses off of the man’s face and then throwing them roughly 50 feet to the other end of the parking lot. “He caught my glasses in his finger and that’s when he threw them,” the alleged victim said. “His finger got caught in my glasses.” The video shows the alleged victim walking toward his glasses to retrieve them when Nelson briefly turns back toward his truck but then quickly turns back toward the alleged victim who stops walking. Nelson then appears to walk several feet to the alleged victim and another verbal exchange ensues with Nelson appearing to take a swipe at the alleged victim’s hand.

entry.title: Simpson successfully adds Peregrine Fund funding in appropriations bill
entry.summary: Curtis Finn takes part in an educational program of the Peregrine Fund World Center for Birds of Prey, which works to conserve birds of prey worldwide, and offers educational programs in addition to its conservation work at its center south of Boise. WASHINGTON — The House Appropriations Committee on Wednesday approved its Transportation, Housing and Urban Development, and Related Agencies Appropriations Act. Among the many earmarks is one that, if ultimately passed, will benefit feathered friends in the Treasure Valley. U.S. Rep. Mike Simpson was successful in including $8.5 million in funding for the Global Conservation Education and Condor Recovery Center Project. This funding will be used to enhance the partnership of Boise State University and the Peregrine Fund by constructing and renovating certain facilities and infrastructure at the World Center for Birds of Prey, located just south of Boise, Simpson’s office said in a press statement. “The Peregrine Fund and Boise State University’s collaborative efforts in raptor conservation, science, and species recovery will position Idaho as a continued leader in this field,” Simpson said in a press statement. “As a longtime supporter of Boise State University, The Peregrine Fund and the critical work that they both do, I am proud to advance funding for the proposed recovery center. “The late Dirk Kempthorne, one of Idaho’s most effective public servants and a dear friend of mine, championed species recovery efforts and strongly supported this project.” Former Gov. Kempthorne, who served Idaho as its 30th governor from 1999 to 2006, died April 24 at the age of 74. He also served as Peregrine Fund board chairman until his passing. “Congressman Simpson has long recognized that The Peregrine Fund’s approach — rigorous science, durable partnerships, and measurable results — is exactly how endangered species get recovered. This investment doesn’t just support the condor; it establishes Idaho as a global center for collaborative conservation excellence and ensures the next generation of scientists carries that model to ecosystems around the world,” Chris Parish, president and CEO of The Peregrine Fund, said. The Transportation, Housing and Urban Development, and Related Agencies Appropriations Act for 2027 has a total discretionary spending allocations of about $92 billion. Other items championed by Simpson include $5.8 million for the Dry Creek Bridge replacement project along U.S. Highway 30 near Murtaugh, as well as $15 million Milner Dam rehabilitation project near Burley. The next step in passing the appropriations act is a full House floor vote, followed by a Senate review and reconciliation. Once the House and Senate pass a bill with the exact same text, it moves on to the president to be signed into law. Trevor Hawes is the executive editor of the Idaho Press. entry.title: Senior center to see significant upgrades thanks to grant award
entry.summary: The Cambridge Midvale Goodrich Senior Center will undergo several improvements thanks to an Idaho Community Development Block Grant. CAMBRIDGE — A community cornerstone will be revamped thanks to a nearly quarter-million-dollar grant. The city of Cambridge announced that it will renovate its longstanding senior center after being awarded a $245,000 Idaho Community Development Block Grant. Established in 1969, the Cambridge Midvale Goodrich Senior Center “delivers essential services including meals, transportation, home care assistance, preventative health screenings, and home-delivered nutrition programs for residents aged 60 and over,” a news release states. The release notes that people aged 60 and older make up nearly 30% of the small town’s population. Cambridge is located about 100 miles north of Boise in Washington County. “This grant is a tremendous victory for our seniors and for our entire community. The Cambridge Midvale Goodrich Senior Center has been a lifeline for generations of residents, and these renovations will ensure it can serve many generations more,” Cambridge Mayor Mark Loveland stated in the release. “We are deeply grateful to the Idaho Department of Commerce for this investment in Cambridge and to Frontier Community Resources for their invaluable partnership in helping us secure it.” Because of a partnership of Frontier Community Resources, “which provided expert technical assistance in developing and submitting the competitive grant application,” the senior center will undergo the following improvements at its 3,350-square-foot facility and adjacent 1,500-square-foot food pantry: “The Cambridge Midvale Goodrich Senior Center is an extraordinary resource for a community that relies on it deeply,” Michele McFarlane, Director of Community and Economic Development at Frontier Community Resources, stated in the release. “Frontier Community Resources is committed to helping Idaho’s rural communities access the funding they need to serve their most vulnerable residents. We are proud to have supported Cambridge in securing this investment and look forward to seeing the impact these renovations will have for years to come.” Madison Guernsey is the News Editor at the Idaho Press. entry.title: Kratom banned in Idaho Falls after six local deaths in 18 months
entry.summary: This Sept. 27, 2017, file photo shows kratom capsules in Albany, N.Y. IDAHO FALLS — The Idaho Falls City Council unanimously approved an ordinance with an effective date of July 1 that prohibits the sale of kratom within the geographical boundaries of the city during Thursday night’s meeting in the City Council Chambers. “I’ve recognized that kratom will continue to be sold online in other cities and counties,” Councilor Michelle Ziel-Dingman said before she made the motion to adopt the ordinance. “But this ordinance makes it clear where our community stands on this drug, and if our ordinance saves one life, as Councilor Jim Freeman said, or serves as a tool for educating our community, I believe this ordinance is therefore appropriate.” All five council members approved the ordinance. Mayor Lisa Burtenshaw said the city will now move forward with an awareness campaign about the ban. “I do sympathize with parents who have no idea that these products are sitting on the shelf,” Burtenshaw said. “That is something that we will address with the Idaho Falls Police and through the mayor’s office and through the school district.” The vote followed the City Council taking official comments from local law enforcement and the Bonneville County coroner about the potential risks of kratom at a work session Monday and hearing additional public comments Thursday night both for and against the proposed ban. Kratom is a plant-based product commonly available in smoke shops, convenience stores and specialty shops. It’s typically promoted as a natural remedy for pain, energy and wellness. However, officials cautioned that the substance can act as a stimulant in small doses and produce opioid-like effects in larger amounts. It is currently an FDA-unregulated drug and legal in the state of Idaho. Bonneville County Coroner Shante Sanchez earlier reported that her office has examined six kratom-related deaths in the past 18 months, most involving young adults. entry.title: Idaho Congressman Fulcher talks Iran War, public land management in phone town hall
entry.summary: U.S. Rep. Russ Fulcher addressed issues with Idahoans during a recent telephone town hall. Originally published June 4 on IdahoCapitalSun.com. Idaho Republican U.S. Rep. Russ Fulcher on Wednesday fielded questions about the war with Iran and his support of transferring management of public lands. Fulcher held a telephone town hall Wednesday evening, answering questions from constituents around the state. He represents Idaho’s 1st congressional district, which covers the North Idaho panhandle and the western side of the state. FULCHER SAYS HE SUPPORTS MORE LOCAL INVOLVEMENT IN FEDERAL PUBLIC LANDS Fulcher, asked about his previous support for exploring the potential transfer of federal public land management to state and local control, said he only supports more local involvement not “transferring title.” Nearly two-thirds of Idaho’s landmass is federal public lands. “I’m talking about how we combine efforts, federal, state, local, municipal, private, in the management decisions of what we have,” Fulcher said. “Because things are so strapped, because we don’t have the ability to manage what’s there, that’s why the fire suppression is so high.” Last December, Fulcher sent a letter to local leaders asking to convene meetings to explore shifting control of federal lands to local entities, the Idaho Capital Sun previously reported. Last year, Fulcher voted in favor of an amendment to what became the “Big Beautiful Bill” that would have sold off thousands of acres of federal lands in Nevada and Utah, the Idaho Press reported. The amendment was later removed. Fulcher and fellow members of Idaho’s congressional delegation, Sens. Mike Crapo and Jim Risch, also voted against a “Public Lands in Public Hands” amendment to block the sale of large swaths of public lands, Utah News Dispatch reported. Idaho U.S. Rep. Mike Simpson was the only delegation member to vote in favor. FULCHER SUPPORTS ISRAEL AS ALLY, AND SOME MILITARY ACTION IN IRAN Two callers asked about U.S. involvement in the war in Iran with Israel. A caller from Meridian asked about a largely symbolic resolution approved earlier that day by the U.S. House to halt military action in Iran. A third caller questioned Fulcher about a proposal to deepen ties with Israeli military forces, including sharing technology and research. One caller said he was “a little hesitant” regarding the U.S.’s financial support for Israel, as well as military action with Iran, noting the rising fuel prices. Fulcher said Israel has been “a very good ally,” especially in regards to intelligence activities in the Middle East. “They’re a friend, they’re another country that’s a friend, and you want to take care of your friends, our friends need to take care of you,” Fulcher said. Fulcher noted that the U.S. provides Israel with about $3.8 billion in foreign aid a year in a 10-year agreement set to expire in 2028. The U.S., with support from Israeli leaders, is trying to wind down that support, Fulcher said. The Washington Post on Wednesday reported that GOP U.S. House members were preparing to introduce a resolution to end the yearly aid, and Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu wrote a letter in support. The foreign aid may be replaced with stronger cooperation among each country’s militaries, Fulcher said. Asked about the section of the draft 2027 National Defense Authorization that would require cooperative efforts among military systems and technology, he said, “it’s a cooperative effort, is my understanding.” “… Stay tuned, and we’ll try to keep people updated as we learn more,” he later added. Fulcher said he backed the U.S. supporting Israeli military action in Iran, if it can prevent the country from gaining nuclear weapons. “If they have one, they’ll use it,” Fulcher said of a nuclear bomb. “But frankly, after that, my preference is to get out. Make sure that they can’t do that, and get out just as quick as we can.”

entry.title: Idaho raw milk illness uptick sparks investigation
entry.summary: Dozens of people have fallen ill after consuming raw milk in recent weeks, and the Idaho Department of Health and Welfare is investigating. The health department said in a news release that nearly 60 people since May 19 have gotten sick after consuming raw (unpasteurized) milk. At least 45 of those people tested positive for campylobacteriosis, a bacterial infection, although not everyone who is sick was tested, IDHW said, adding that investigation and interviews of people reported as ill are ongoing and additional illness may be identified. The Idaho Division of Public Health is collaborating with Panhandle District Health, Southwest District Health, Central District Health, Southeastern Idaho Public Health, South Central Public Health, and Eastern Idaho Public Health on the investigation. Raw, unpasteurized dairy products can contain bacteria that make people sick, particularly young children, pregnant women, the elderly, and those who are immunocompromised, IDHW says. Pasteurization kills nearly all the germs that can exist in raw milk while maintaining nutritional benefits. The Department of Health and Welfare notes that common symptoms of campylobacteriosis infection include: Symptoms usually start two to five days after exposure and last about one week, and some people may develop complications that last longer. Anyone who is experiencing symptoms after consuming raw milk or raw milk products is encouraged to promptly seek medical care. IDHW reports that most people who were sick reported consuming raw milk from two different milking operations — one in northern Idaho and one in southern Idaho. Investigation is ongoing to identify potential batches of concern and test milk samples. Both milking operations are working in collaboration with the department and local public health agencies to identify and fix any potential sources of contamination, IDHW said. Madison Guernsey is the News Editor at the Idaho Press. Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. Sign up now! entry.title: U.S. House approves Idaho Rep. Fulcher’s bill to increase geothermal leasing
entry.summary: A plaque on the Hoff Building in downtown Boise signifying the building uses geothermal heat. Originally published June 4 on IdahoCapitalSun.com. The U.S. House on Tuesday approved a bill brought by Idaho Republican Rep. Russ Fulcher to increase geothermal energy leasing on public lands. Fulcher sponsored H.R. 1687, the “Committing Leases for Energy Access Now” or “CLEAN” Act with cosponsors, Reps. Celeste Maloy (R-Utah), Lauren Boebert (R-Colo.), Nicholas Begich (R-Alaska), and Susie Lee (D-Nev.). The House approved the bill in a voice vote, and it will go to the U.S. Senate for consideration. “Geothermal is a clean, abundant, and dependable energy resource that can help meet America’s rapidly rising power demands,” Fulcher said in a press release. “However, the lack of consistent geothermal lease sales and the slow federal permitting process result in timelines longer than many other energy projects. The bipartisan CLEAN Act will address these barriers and unlock opportunities to explore and develop this renewable energy source. The bill amends the Geothermal Steam Act of 1970 to require the secretary of the interior to hold a lease sale for geothermal projects every year, instead of every two years. It would also add requirements that the secretary hold a replacement lease sale if any are canceled or missed in a calendar year. Geothermal uses the heat from inside the earth to provide heat to buildings or generate electricity. The energy resource is particularly abundant in the West, according to the Bureau of Land Management, which oversees geothermal leasing on federal lands and on the 700 million acres where the U.S. owns subsurface mineral estate. Boise is home to the nation’s first geothermal district heating system, established in 1892. Idaho’s Capitol is the only geothermally heated capitol building in the U.S., according to the press release from Fulcher. The Raft River Geothermal Power Plant in Cassia County produces 10 megawatts of electricity, according to the director of the University of Idaho’s Energy Institute, John Kumm. In April, the BLM sought public input on a proposed geothermal project totaling 114,000 acres in Washington, Owyhee, Twin Falls, Cassia, Clark and Lemhi counties. entry.title: JFAC takes lead on Rural Health Transformation Program after previous bills stalled
entry.summary: The door to the Joint Finance Appropriations Committee (JFAC) meeting room at the Idaho State Capitol, Wednesday, March 4. BOISE — With past efforts to provide direction on the Rural Health Transformation Program floundering this session, the Joint Finance-Appropriations Committee took it upon themselves Tuesday to advance guardrails for how hundreds of millions in health care grants are to be spent. The Rural Health Transformation Program (RHTP) was established last year following the passage of the One Big Beautiful Bill. The $50 billion in funding for the program is scheduled to be distributed to all 50 states in $10 billion increments from 2026 through 2030. Idaho was awarded $929 million over this time frame in increments of $186 million that will each be available for two years before expiring. A prior survey issued by the Department of Health and Welfare identified the top three uses for Idaho’s funds as being recruiting and retaining clinical workforce talent; assisting rural communities in identifying their health care needs across the system including emergency, pre-hospital and outpatient care; and improving prevention and chronic disease management. To prepare for the allocation of this funding, JFAC advanced legislation to establish the Idaho rural health transformation fund comprised of these federal funds that are to be distributed according to recommendations from a new rural health transformation committee. Though several bills to provide oversight on this funding have been presented by legislators this session, the key sticking point has been the makeup of this committee, which has grown from five members to nine members across a series of bills originating in both the House and the Senate. The legislation approved by JFAC states the committee will consist of nine members with four coming from the Senate and appointed by the Senate President Pro Tempore (currently Sen. Kelly Anthon, R-Rupert), four members coming from the House and appointed by the Speaker of the House (currently Rep. Mike Moyle, R-Star) and one non-voting member elected by the governor. Alex Williamson, senior analyst for the Legislative Services Office, said the committee makeup mirrors what House Bill 916 intended to establish. Though that bill unanimously passed the House earlier this month, it did not receive a hearing from the Senate Health and Welfare Committee. The Senate instead voted to support Senate Bill 1264, which included specifications for the nine-member committee to include three representatives and three senators representing rural districts, defined as those representing districts with a population center of no more than 20,000 people. Though not including this rural representation stipulation, the legislation from JFAC includes a provision from the Senate bill requiring committee members have access to data on RHTP including awards received or created by the Idaho Department of Health and Welfare. Sen. Kevin Cook, R-Idaho Falls, who was the floor sponsor for Senate Bill 1264, questioned why the Senate bill was not set to have a hearing in the House after passing the Senate on Monday. Rep. Josh Tanner, R-Eagle, who co-chairs JFAC, said House leadership stated the bill was not going to be heard and the bill before JFAC aligned with their recommendations. Cook responded that the removal of the requirement for rural representation on the committee would result in legislators representing areas serviced by urban health care giants St. Luke’s and Saint Alphonsus making funding decisions for rural hospitals, rural health care and rural constituents. “I think that is just really, really wrong,” Cook said, “… why would you want city people representing rural?” After Cook voiced these concerns, JFAC unanimously approved the legislation establishing the nine-member oversight committee without rural district requirements, which will go before both the House and the Senate for a wider floor vote. In separate funding consideration for the first and second years of the RHTP, JFAC aligned with recommendations from Gov. Brad Little to allocate resources to 12 “limited service” full-time positions — which will be in place for the duration of the federal program — along with $3.7 million in federal funds for fiscal year 2026 for a portion of this year’s award. In a 14-4 decision the committee voted to align with the governor and allocate these resources for the current fiscal year. A subsequent request for $295.4 million — comprised of funds for the first and second year of federal awards and the aforementioned 12 employees — was approved for fiscal year 2027 in a 15-4 vote. Williamson with the Legislative Services Office said the supplemental funding allows the department to begin moving on the federal program and start appropriating the funds for different rural health objectives. “The state does have an obligation to obligate those funds for year one by October of this year,” Williamson said. “So allowing a supplemental would give them a bit longer of a runway on getting those dollars out the door.” Royce McCandless is the Statehouse reporter and covers Idaho politics. You can email him at . Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. Sign up now!

entry.title: City of Boise takes down Pride flag after Gov. Little signs flag restrictions into law
entry.summary: Flags fly outside Bose City Hall, including the Pride flag and flag honoring organ and tissue donors, in April 2025. The Pride flag was taken down Tuesday by Mayor Lauren McLean to comply with a new state law that restricts what flags can be flown on city and county property. BOISE — After more than a decade of flying the Pride flag outside city hall, Boise Mayor Lauren McLean ordered the LGBTQ+ Pride flag be taken down to comply with a new law placing significant restrictions on what flags can be flown on city and county property and imposing steep fines for violations. Gov. Brad Little signed into law HB 561 on Tuesday, restricting what flags cities and counties are able to fly as part of an effort to end the flying of the LGBTQ+ Pride flag in Boise. The law went into effect immediately after its signing. “This law seeks to erase both the values we hold dear and people who are integral to the fabric of our community,” McLean said in a news release from the city of Boise. “We will continue to celebrate the vibrancy of our community, the diversity of our residents, and our North Star of being a safe and welcoming city for everyone.” The legislation authored by Rep. Ted Hill, R-Eagle, restricts cities and counties to flying the United States flag, official flags of any state in the United States, official flags of military branches and units, the POW/MIA flag, the official flag of recognized Indian tribes and the official flags of countries other than the United States for special occasions, with exceptions for nations the U.S. is engaged in hostile action against. The legislation was amended several times this legislative session to allow for carve-outs for the flying of the Canadian flag in Bonners Ferry and the flying of the Basque autonomous community flag after legislators and members of the public took issue with these flags potentially being unable to fly on city- and county-owned property after several decades of doing so. McLean stated her decision to take down the flag on Tuesday was the result of the legislation’s substantial financial penalty, which would require the city to pay $2,000 per violating flag for each day they are displayed. Before a civil fine can be imposed, violating entities are notified by the attorney general, who provides 10 days to correct the violation before civil action is taken. If not corrected in this time frame — or if the entity has had similar violations in the past — the attorney general can take civil action immediately. This enforcement provision was notably absent from the state’s previous flag law and led to a public exchange of letters between McLean and Idaho Attorney General Raúl Labrador after the city continued to fly the Pride flag along with a flag honoring organ and tissue donors. After this dispute, McLean — with support from city council — designated the Pride flag and the organ donor flag as “official” flags of the city of Boise to comply with the previous law. This option will no longer be available to Boise or any city moving forward as cities and counties can only use flags established as “official” prior to Jan. 1, 2023. As for the reasoning behind this date, Hill previously said city flags have become a “political enterprise” in the time since. McLean stated in the release the city was reviewing “all legal avenues and considering next steps” in light of the new law being enacted and had a “responsibility to respond with both conviction and care.” ”To our LGBTQ+ friends, family, and neighbors: you are an essential part of Boise,” McLean stated. “You are welcome here. You belong here. And no law can change that.” Editor''s Note: This article has been corrected to accurately identify the bill number of the new flag legislation. It is House Bill 561. Royce McCandless is the Statehouse reporter and covers Idaho politics. You can email him at . Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. Sign up now! To view our latest e-Edition, click the image on the left. Search the complete digital archives for all papers in the Pioneer News Group. Idaho Press 1618 N. Midland Blvd. Nampa, ID 83651 Phone: 208-467-9251 Email: Contact Us

entry.title: Committee sends ICE agreement bill for amendment
entry.summary: A bill that would have required law enforcement agencies in Idaho to apply for cooperative agreements with U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement has been halted in the Senate. BOISE — The Senate State Affairs Committee opted to differ with law enforcement in sending one immigration enforcement bill for amendment, but they aligned with law enforcement’s concerns by rejecting another. The immigration enforcement bill advanced to the amending order by the committee on Monday is a renewed effort to require local law enforcement — including police departments and sheriffs’ offices — to apply for cooperation programs with U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement. These programs, known as 287(g) agreements, allow local law enforcement to be deputized as immigration enforcement agents to enforce federal law. House Bill 659 previously died in committee after receiving negative testimony from local law enforcement who argued the bill would result in officers being taken off patrols and restrict the ability for these agencies to make decisions that are in their best interest, particularly as federal objectives change significantly with time from one administration to the next. Sen. Kelly Anthon, R-Rupert, who sponsored the legislation, said his aim was to “bridge the gap” between the previous legislation and law enforcement concerns. The key change in the legislation pertains to the application process. Idaho law enforcement agencies would be required to apply for 287(g) agreements with ICE, even if such agreements can’t be fulfilled. However, under the bill, participation is only required “to the extent that the necessary resources for participation are reasonably available to the law enforcement agency.” If this is the case, county commissioners or city councils are directed to issue a finding of fact for why entering into such an agreement would hinder their law enforcement agency. Several of the law enforcement leaders who testified against the previous bill came before the Senate committee Monday voicing the same concerns from the previous bill. Canyon County Sheriff Kieran Donahue said the new bill fails to eliminate the mandate that law enforcement apply for these agreements, presenting a “real danger for the state of Idaho” in requiring law enforcement to enter into these agreements and in turn dictate how Idaho law enforcement is utilized. Ada County Sheriff Matt Clifford, speaking on behalf of the Idaho Sheriffs Association, said the association was opposed to the legislation on the grounds it would take away the ability for law enforcement agencies to make the best decisions for themselves. He referred to a prior Memorandum of Understanding, or cooperative agreement, with the U.S. Marshals under the Biden administration that would have required local law enforcement to give all evidence from a critical incident to the federal government including body camera footage, reports and evidence that would have prevented local law enforcement from investigating a shooting. Though new agreements with the U.S. Marshals have since been made under the Trump administration, Clifford said the ability to previously say “‘I’m not signing that,’” would no longer be permissible when it came to ICE. “This takes that away,” Clifford said. “There are back doors written into here for inabilities, not for, ‘this is not good for the community anymore.’” Sen. Ben Adams, R-Nampa, said his support for the legislation was instead born out of a desire to respond to Idahoans calling for more robust immigration enforcement in this state. “This approach — although not seen as the best move by all — is a way to ensure to the citizens of Idaho that their law enforcement is proactive in helping the federal government address that issue,” Adams said. “That’s what I see what this is about.” In a 6-3 vote, the committee voted to send Anthon’s bill for amendment prior to being debated on the Senate floor. Anthon said the key part of the bill he is looking to clarify is when the Federal Tort Claim Act applies to allow private parties to sue the U.S. government. Additional language would be needed to outline the Idaho officers are subject to the state’s own tort claim or liability legislation even when working on behalf of the federal government, Anthon said. Committee rejects conditional release to ICE Sen. Lori Den Hartog, R-Meridian introduced new legislation allowing individuals in the custody of the Idaho Department of Correction subject to a federal immigration detainer to be released to ICE prior the completion of their sentence from crimes committed. Under the bill, the IDOC director would be able to reach an agreement with ICE to move an offender to an ICE facility prior to the expiration of the offenders’ prison sentence, with exceptions for life sentences.

entry.title: Idaho Senate rejects 'tool of weaponization' bill granting AG greater power
entry.summary: BOISE — The state Senate resoundingly rejected legislation Thursday that would have greatly expanded the powers of the Idaho Attorney General to pursue legal action to remove public officials and employees across state and local government. House Bill 896 was initially brought forward by House Speaker Mike Moyle and allows the attorney general to serve as a legal backstop in case a public official, public employee or government entity violates law without enforcement mechanisms. This situation arose when Boise Mayor McLean and Idaho Attorney General Raúl Labrador exchanged public letters over the city continuing to fly the LGBTQ+ Pride flag after being prohibited under a 2025 law that lacked an enforcement mechanism. Though the lack of enforcement was highlighted by McLean at the time, the omission was addressed in this year’s flag law. In the event similar situations were to arise in the future, House Bill 896 stipulated that any instance where a public official were to act in contradiction to Idaho prohibitions, the president pro tempore of the Senate, the speaker of the House of Representatives and the chair of a board of county commissioners can make referrals to the attorney general’s office regarding potential prohibition violations. If the attorney general believes a violation is occurring, they are required to provide the potentially violating party with 14 days notice to state intent to correct a violation or deny the violation was taking place. If the violation is not corrected, the attorney general can take the issue to court to for a decision on whether truly unlawful action is taking place. In addition to turning to the court to seek compliance, the attorney general would have also been allowed to seek to disqualify individuals from public office or public employment if proving someone “willfully” violated state prohibitions. If this were the case, the court would have been able to bar someone from seeking public office or employment in the state for up to five years. In an 8-27 vote, the Senate placed a definitive stamp of disapproval for this course of action being made available. Outside of Sen. Todd Lakey, R-Nampa, who served as the floor sponsor for the bill, debate from legislators from across the political spectrum was entirely negative. Members of both parties raised concerns the legislation would consolidate power in the state, lead to paranoia amongst public officials and employees and present a fundamentally unnecessary solution in the face of existing oversight mechanisms. Sen. Treg Bernt, R-Meridian, said the bill would allow the state to “supersede local decision making” by “stripping communities” of their ability to manage local issues. In addition to intruding on the relationship between state and local government, Bernt said the bill would bring “unprecedented authority” and investigatory power into the attorney general’s office. “This consolidation places critical decisions affecting freedom and democracy in the hands of a selected few, rather than dispersing power as intended in a representative government,” Bernt said. “This ship fundamentally alters the balance of power in the state of Idaho — I have grave concerns over this.” Sen. Ali Rabe, D-Boise, questioned why the legislation was necessary with the safeguards already in place. Even in bills that lack enforcement mechanisms, Rabe underlined there are mechanisms in place to “keep our elected officials” in check, including the Idaho Bribery and Corrupt Influences Act and the Idaho Ethics in Government Act, both of which govern the conduct of public officials. “I worry also it could put us at odds with each other and make us kind of paranoid against each other,” Rabe said. “It seems pretty easy in here to just send a referral from one elected official to another about anyone over anything. … I believe that it could potentially be abused in a lot of ways.” Pointing to the provision of the legislation that could bar people from public office for up to five years, Sen. Josh Kohl, R-Twin Falls, said blocking individuals from seeking office puts the state on “thin ice” in maintaining constitutional protections. “It’s just a tool of potential weaponization, and people could use this to target people they just don’t like or are feuding with. … You could imagine 1,000 scenarios where people could use this to target someone,” Sen. Brian Lenney, R-Nampa, said. Royce McCandless is the Statehouse reporter and covers Idaho politics. You can email him at . Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. Sign up now! To view our latest e-Edition, click the image on the left. Search the complete digital archives for all papers in the Pioneer News Group. Idaho Press 1618 N. Midland Blvd. Nampa, ID 83651 Phone: 208-467-9251 Email: Contact Us

entry.title: Caldwell mayor attempts to veto resolutions, but has no legal authority
entry.summary: A car crosses the railroad tracks at North 5th Avenue through Caldwell earlier this year. Mayor Eric Phillips on Wednesday, April 1, issued a veto of the quiet zone ordinance but the city attorney said in a legal opinion Phillips does not have the legal authority to do that. A train approaches the North 10th Avenue overpass as it passes through downtown Caldwell, Monday, Feb. 9. The railroad quiet zone saga has reemerged in Caldwell following Mayor Eric Phillips’ failed attempt to issue two vetoes on Wednesday, which have no legal effect and binding authority, according to a legal opinion by the city of Caldwell’s attorney. The city was set to begin the process of implementing a railroad quiet zone at seven of its crossings in central Caldwell following the 5-1 vote on two resolutions at a special city council meeting on March 25, which were to accept a bid proposal for the railroad quiet zone construction project — which would install security features at designated railroad crossings — and to approve and authorize the implementation of a railroad quiet zone. But Phillips — who was sworn in to lead the city in January — tried shooting down the council’s votes citing city code. In a legal opinion requested by Council Member Scott Tilmant and sent to the Idaho Press by Council Member Mike Dittenber, the city’s attorney, Oscar Klaas, referenced both city and Idaho code, which only allows a mayor to veto ordinances, not resolutions — therefore not giving Phillips legal authority to veto the quiet zone resolutions which were passed by the council. “... it is the legal opinion of the City Attorney that the veto authority of the mayor is limited to ordinances and does not extend to resolutions of the City Council. Therefore, the “vetoes” issued by the Mayor on April 1, 2026, are of no legal effect and have no binding authority,” Klaas said in his opinion. In a drawn-out statement through a city-issued press release, Phillips laid out his reasonings for his two attempted vetoes, pointing to ongoing public safety and fiscal concerns, and a lack of immediate necessity compared to other safety priorities needed throughout the city. “My responsibility as mayor is to ensure that decisions made by the city prioritize public safety, fiscal responsibility, and the long-term well-being of our community. At this time, I do not believe these actions meet that standard,” Phillips stated in a closing statement regarding Resolution No. 095-26 on the acceptance of a bid proposal. While an actual veto to a law by a mayor could be overridden by council at the next council meeting based on Idaho law, which in this instance would be done on Monday, April 6, Caldwell council members are not required to address or consider passing the quiet zone resolutions over the vetoes issued by the mayor, unless the council elects to do so, according to Klaas. “Any ordinance vetoed by the mayor may be passed over his veto by a vote of one-half (1/2) plus one (1) of the members of the full council,” according to state law. A designated quiet zone area does not require train engineers to routinely sound horns but still allows them to in the case of an emergency, according to federal regulations. Cities must install approved safety precautions by a diagnostic team — including representatives from the Federal Railroad Administration and Union Pacific — before a quiet zone can be implemented. There are over 1,000 existing quiet zones across the country that cover over 5,500 crossings. The decision to implement a railroad quiet zone at seven of Caldwell’s crossings, which are Fifth Avenue, Kimball Avenue, Ninth Avenue, 12th Avenue, 21st Avenue, Linden Street and Ustick Road, has been an ongoing topic that has caused heated debates over the last few months. And while the city has considered adding a quiet zone to its daily 17 trains for over a decade, the concept made progress last summer when the council approved the allocation of $400,000 in the FY 2026 budget to be used to establish a quiet zone. Project costs currently sit under budget at $238,000. Bruce Mills, the city’s deputy public works director of transportation, has researched the project for the last two years. Council members had a longer-than-intended wait to cast their votes for the quiet zone agreement and construction bid for the added safety features which were going to include a raised median curb, warning sign installations, and closing of access points close to the tracks. Phillips moved the items as old business in hopes to have further discussion with the council, as he made it clear that he was not in agreement with the timing of a quiet zone and raised concerns regarding pedestrian safety. But the council’s patience was cut short when Tilmant, a first-year council member, made a motion at the March 16 city council meeting to move the vote to the soonest upcoming council meeting. Dittenber said he feels disheartened by the divisiveness and questioned when the quiet zone saga will come to an end. “What I would hope was that this divisiveness, not only amongst council but also amongst the community, will come to an end where we can collectively work together to do great things for the great city of Caldwell,” Dittenber said. Dittenber believes that if there was a vote on a quiet zone again, it would still pass with a 5-1 vote to override the mayor’s veto. Currently, the mayor''s vetoes on the quiet zone resolutions are listed under old business for Monday’s city council meeting, but it is unclear if the council will choose to override them or not. Mariela Esquivel-Rodriguez is a reporter for the Idaho Press focused on Caldwell and Canyon County. You can email her at Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. Sign up now!

entry.title: Bill requiring local law enforcement to apply for agreements with ICE halted in the Senate
entry.summary: A bill that would have required law enforcement agencies in Idaho to apply for cooperative agreements with U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement has been halted in the Senate. BOISE — The Idaho Senate has halted the advancement of a bill that would have required law enforcement agencies to apply for cooperative agreements with U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement. Following the bill’s passage out of the House on Thursday, Sen. Mark Harris, R-Soda Springs, motioned to not concur with the House’s amendments to the legislation, preventing it from advancing on grounds that it violates Senate Rule 28(D). Under this rule, a bill cannot be amended to remove or incorporate provisions of another bill already pending in the Senate. Senate Bill 1247 was originally the Idaho e-verify act to require all public agencies to implement the federal e-verify program, which confirms the eligibility to work in the United States for new employees. The House amendment retooled the bill entirely to effectively swap a pending ICE cooperation bill with the stalled e-verify legislation. By unanimous consent, the Senate opted to not advance the reworked bill. Under the amended Senate bill, city and county law enforcement would be required to apply for cooperation agreements with U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement. These arrangements — known as section 287(g) memorandums of agreement — allow local law enforcement officers to be trained and deputized under programs that permit limited enforcement of federal immigration law. These programs function according to three separate models. A jail enforcement model allows officers to identify and process removable undocumented immigrants with either pending or active criminal charges. A task force model allows officers to have limited immigration enforcement authority in the performance of their routine police duties and participate in ICE-led task forces. A warrant service officer model allows officers to serve executive administrative warrants on undocumented immigrants in custody. Under each of these models, individual officers would need to participate in training and/or receive certification from ICE in order to be deputized to enforce federal immigration law. In cases where a law enforcement agency is “unable” to enter into a section 287(g) agreement, the law enforcement agency is required to publish a statement on the reasons for its inability to enter into an agreement as well as the alternative efforts made to cooperate with “enforcement and removal operation of immigration and customs enforcement.” According to the ICE website, a variety of sheriffs offices such as Kootenai and Owyhee counties are already engaged in some capacity with at least one of these three agreements. At the direction of Gov. Brad Little, the Idaho State Police also entered a 287(g) agreement last year to transport unauthorized immigrants convicted of crimes to deportation centers. This bill is a new version of House Bill 659, which passed out of the House last month before being held in Senate committee in a one-vote margin. In the public hearing before the House and the Senate, the measure was opposed by the police chiefs and county sheriffs who testified. Canyon County Sheriff Kieran Donahue, who testified on the previous bill on behalf of the Idaho Sheriffs Association, said sheriffs in the state were "absolutely opposed" to the legislation due to it mandating what sheriffs can and can''t do. He highlighted law enforcement would be on the hook for backfill and other expenses associated with officers departing for ICE-led training and would result in fewer officers to participate in daily patrol duties. In addition to testifying on previous renditions of the bill, Ada County Sheriff Matt Clifford issued a statement last week underscoring his office already cooperates with ICE on a daily basis to share arrest lists and comply with lawful requests to hold individuals for deportation. Clifford also emphasized that he has been able to align or decouple his agency depending on when federal objectives align with the needs of the county. "In my role as sheriff, I have made decisions to end partnerships with federal agencies when their demands or direction were overreaching and no longer in the best interest of Ada County," Clifford stated. "A forced relationship with ICE under a mandated program gives me no such option when participation begins to erode what is best for our community." During debate on the House floor, Rep. Britt Raybould, R-Rexburg, questioned why the legislature was seeking to force law enforcement’s hand in the matter when agencies are already sharing relevant information with ICE or participating in 287(g) agreements themselves. Local law enforcement can already enter 287(g) agreements if they feel doing so is in the best interest of their department and community, whereas this legislation would make applying for these agreements no longer subject to agency discretion, Raybould said. “The idea that we would allow and encourage the federal overriding of the discretion of our local law enforcement does not sit well with me,” Raybould said.

entry.title: Legislature passes bill making ACHD races partisan and countywide starting in 2028
entry.summary: This photo provided by the Ada County Highway District shows a road managed by the county agency. Starting in 2028, ACHD elections will be partisan and countywide, instead of by district. BOISE — The Idaho Legislature approved a bill Wednesday that would make Ada County Highway District elections partisan and countywide. Similar changes to the body were considered last session before they died in committee. The bill was fast-tracked through the legislature Wednesday, passing out of the Senate in the afternoon and sent to the House for a hearing later that evening. Late Wednesday night, the House passed the legislation in a 47-20 vote. The bill is now poised to cap off a multi-year effort to transform Idaho’s only countywide highway district into a partisan body. Though ACHD would keep its current five sub-district arrangement, commissioners would no longer be elected solely by the residents of their respective district and instead be required to participate in partisan elections with associated primaries after operating as a non-partisan body since its inception. Once signed into law, the changes would go into effect for the 2028 election cycle. In her presentation of the legislation on the Senate floor, Sen. Lori Den Hartog, R-Meridian, said the changes would make ACHD elections mirror those of the Ada County Commissioners, who are required to live in a certain district but are elected by every resident of the county. Den Hartog said the shift to countywide elections would allow for the voices of individuals who travel across the county to be heard by commissioners representing the different sub-districts. With several state highways running through the county, Den Hartog said it was important to consider the regional needs of the county as ACHD projects frequently bump up against the highways under the Idaho Transportation Department’s jurisdiction. “As you think about how that state system interacts with the local system here, it’s really important to consider all of those regional needs and make sure that the voice of everyone in Ada County is heard at the ballot box,” Den Hartog said. Alexis Pickering, vice president of the ACHD commissioner board, testified against the legislation during the Wednesday hearing in the House. For commissioners on the body, the five districts of approximately 100,000 are already double to size of legislative districts in Idaho. Shifting the voter base to Ada County’s population, which exceeds 500,000, would make ACHD commissioners beholden to a population the size of a congressional district while fulfilling part-time positions, Pickering said. Miranda Gold, ACHD commission president, testified on behalf of the commission and similarly spoke against the legislation she perceived as marking a sea change for how the commission operates. When the body was formed with the approval of Ada County voters in the 1970s, Gold said the thinking at the time was countywide elections with sub-district residency requirements would “dilute representation” in parts of the county and change the “tone and tenor of the commission.” ”A candidate living in Kuna would need to campaign in Star, and a commissioner could be elected by 80% of voters who do not live in their district,” Gold said. In introducing partisanship and districtwide elections to the body, commissioners will be turned into “full-time fundraisers,” introducing moneyed interests to these races and politicizing transportation projects, Gold said. Echoing Den Hartog, Rep. Jason Monks, R-Meridian, maintained on the House floor that the changes were simply to mirror the arrangement for the county commissioners. ”We’re trying to say that ACHD operates very similar to your county commissioners,” Monks said, “and they should be treated the same.” Rep. Steve Berch, D-Boise, testified twice against the legislation on the House floor and said rejecting the legislation was a matter of remaining consistent for the legislature. He noted in the past sessions the legislature enacted requirements that city council members in cities of 100,000 or more be elected by and live in particular districts. ”If we want to be consistent, we should be requiring the Ada County Commissioners be elected by the district that they’re in, instead of districtwide,” Berch said. Royce McCandless is the Statehouse reporter and covers Idaho politics. You can email him at .

entry.title: JFAC votes to withhold more than $50 million from general fund, opting for larger bottom line
entry.summary: Legislators at work on the floor of the Idaho Senate as the end of the legislative session draws near Tuesday, March 31, 2026. BOISE — Throughout the legislative session, leadership on the Joint Finance-Appropriations Committee has framed 2% cuts to state agencies for the next fiscal year as a necessary action to ensure the state’s budget was in a “structurally sound” position. On Wednesday, however, the committee voted to have tens of million in funds held in the state’s Budget Stabilization Fund that would have otherwise been automatically transferred to bolster the coming year’s bottom line. Existing code outlining the function of the Budget Stabilization Fund, also known as Idaho’s rainy day fund, states the balance shall not exceed 15% of the total general fund for the ending fiscal year. For fiscal year 2027, this amount would be based upon the fiscal year 2026 revenue, which JFAC projected at $5.51 billion. If exceeding this 15% threshold, the state controller is directed to transfer this money to the general fund unless the legislature acts to reduce the amount of the transfer, which JFAC initiated on Wednesday. This stabilization fund balance currently sits at $880.2 million. When compared to a projected general fund revenue of $5.51 billion for fiscal year 2026, this amounts to a difference of $53.7 million beyond the 15% cap. “It makes no sense to me that we would set aside code and not put this money in the general fund, especially when we have cut budgets to the bone and then some,” Sen. Janie Ward-Engelking, D-Boise, said. This amount of budget stabilization funds that would have otherwise been moved automatically is well above the $30.1 million in ongoing 2% cuts JFAC initially approved for most state agencies in fiscal year 2027. Though several restorations, including those for wildfire suppression, were fully or partially enacted by the body in the time since, this was not true for every agency. In the case of Idaho’s colleges and universities, JFAC voted against restoring a 2% ongoing cut, or $7.3 million general fund reduction for these institutions. “We should have been budgeting for this on the green sheet from the start of session,” Keith Bybee, budget and policy division manager of the Legislative Services Office said in reference to the daily budget update form issued by LSO. “… With the revenue number that the legislature adopted, that would have triggered an automatic transfer from the budget stabilization fund in 2027 back to the general fund.” Bybee said this has not been an issue until the last two sessions when the legislature saw hundreds of millions of dollars in budget surpluses that resulted in this stabilization fund hitting the 15% cap. These were both years that did not see broad agency cuts to state agencies, as was the case this legislative session. Sen. Melissa Wintrow, D-Boise, questioned why the body wouldn’t want the excess funds automatically transferring to the general fund, given the budget constraints of this session. Bybee responded it was a policy decision by the body to leave excess funds on the bottom line for the coming fiscal year. Last week JFAC advanced a series of cash transfers that will only take place if the state’s general fund balance falls below $150 million. JFAC co-chairs Sen. Scott Grow, R-Eagle, and Rep. Josh Tanner, R-Eagle, said the intent is to ensure the state has a “cushion” heading into next year. Sen. Jim Woodward, R-Sagle, took issue with the committee’s course of action and said the 15% cap on the budget stabilization fund was in place to ensure current taxpayers have their dollars appropriately utilized in the present. ”We are asking today’s taxpayers to take care of tomorrow’s taxpayers’ problems,” Woodward said. “The people who are putting money in this budget stabilization fund today may not be Idahoans when it gets spent.” Despite reservations voiced by several committee members on not allowing for the automatic transfer to take place, JFAC voted 16-4 to hold the excess budget stabilization funds for the next fiscal year. Ward-Engelking, Wintrow, Rep. Brooke Green, D-Boise, and Rep. Rod Furniss, R-Rigby, each voted against. Royce McCandless is the Statehouse reporter and covers Idaho politics. You can email him at . Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. Sign up now! To view our latest e-Edition, click the image on the left. entry.title: A tearful Boise City Council declares Trans Day of Visibility
entry.summary: Boise Mayor Lauren McLean responds to the crowd at the Boise Pride parade in 2023. A heaviness set the tone of Tuesday’s Boise City Council meeting, on the heels of anti-LGBTQ+ legislation being signed into Idaho law, as Boise declared March 31 as the Trans Day of Visibility. Council members spoke through tears at the special meeting as they reflected on the importance of allyship following the passage of a flag ban earlier that day. After over a decade of the Pride flag flying outside Boise City Hall, Gov. Brad Little signed an amended law to restrict what flags cities and counties can display. The bill, HB 561, comes after previous attempts to outlaw the Pride flag on city and county property in the 2025 legislative session. Skirting around the ban, Mayor Lauren McLean and the city council continued flying the flag due to a lack of enforcement mechanisms in the 2025 legislation. The updated legislation will now enforce a $2,000 fine for each day an unauthorized flag is displayed. Acceptable flags include the U.S. flag, state flags, military flags, the POW/MIA flag, flags of Indian tribes, and country flags for special occasions. On Tuesday, McLean spoke to a packed room. Over the past year, she has stood firmly in the city’s right to show support for the LGBTQ+ community. “I hope that knowing that you are loved, seen and welcomed by this city enables you to participate in the fabric of this community,” McLean said. She described the day as a sad one for the people who call Boise home, but welcomed the Trans Day of Visibility proclamation as a way to show support for a marginalized population. Tuesday also saw the signing of HB 752, a bill that criminalizes the use of public facilities that do not align with a person’s “biological sex.” A repeat offender will be subject to a felony charge. “They are seeking to divide us by targeting the most vulnerable among us,” McLean said about lawmakers. “There’s little we can do at the state and national level, but there is much that can be done here at home.” McLean choked up as she discussed the future of Idaho’s next generation, describing mothers who have to watch their children leave the state. She seemingly referred to the strained state of Idaho’s obstetrics care and abortion rights. Idaho has some of the most restrictive abortion laws in the nation. With the overturning of Roe v. Wade by the U.S. Supreme Court in 2022, an abortion trigger ban quickly went into effect. “I thought about my own kid, and their questions of whether or not their friends are welcome here,” McLean said. “Whether as a woman, my daughter will be able to have children here in a healthy and safe way.” Council Member Colin Nash reflected on his previous experience as a representative for District 16 in the Idaho House. He resigned in 2024, citing his intent to focus more on city council. “Often in the legislature, it felt like casting your pearls before swine,” Nash said. Nash spoke about his wife’s cousin, Shay, who killed herself a few years ago after a lack of acceptance for identifying with the LGBTQ+ community. As a representative at the time Nash wanted to share her story in the legislature, but, as he expected from previous experience, “it just didn’t resonate, their hearts were hardened.” “Shay was a lovely soul,” Nash said, speaking through tears. “... I’m not asking you to empathize, but if someone feels something so deeply that they would go to those ends, can we just give them a little bit more understanding? A little bit more sympathy?” Sporting a rainbow sticker on her blazer, Council Pro Tem Kathy Corless spoke on the importance of visibility. While she identifies herself as a longtime LGBTQ+ ally, Corless said she has realized a need to be more transparent. “This isn’t the Idaho I grew up in; people are under attack,” Corless said. “This legislation that is coming out feels mean. Spiteful.” Council members encouraged locals to show their support by flying their own Pride flags.

entry.title: Sheriffs echo bill concerns: 'We do not do ICE's job'
entry.summary: Ada County Sheriff Matt Clifford, right, speaks to the Ada County Commissioners during a public interview at the Ada County Courthouse on Wednesday, June 30, 2021 in Boise. BOISE — Several law enforcement officials testified at the Capitol on Monday in opposition to legislation they said would thrust the responsibilities of the federal government and U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement onto local agencies. The opposition arrived in response to a hearing for Senate Bill 1442. If passed into law, city, county and state law enforcement would be required to determine the immigration status and nationality of individuals during investigations and report statistics on undocumented immigrant interactions with law enforcement. Each would be required even if an arrest doesn’t take place. The legislation sponsored by Sen. Kelly Anthon, R-Rupert, is a new rendition of House Bill 660 which passed out of the House earlier in March but has since stalled in the Senate. Senate Bill 1142 now heads to the Senate floor with much of the prior’s bill language — and opposition from law enforcement — along with reporting requirements for refugees resettled in Idaho. This additional section will apply to any entity in the state contracted with the federal government to engage in resettlement services in Idaho. If passed into law, each organization would be required to provide the legislature with an annual audit for the services they provide to refugees. This includes an audit of the number of refugees served, the nationality and gender of refugees served, the language abilities of refugees served, the counties and municipalities where refugees served are housed and the number of housing units taken by refugees served. Sen. Melissa Wintrow, D-Boise, noted that since these organizations are already working with the federal government, this information is already being reported. She questioned, however, whether the reporting requirements around which county or municipality refugees are housed and how many housing units were used was needed. Anthon responded that these metrics would not include addresses or where the specific housing units are and would instead provide general data. ”I’ve always thought in my mind that if we’re bringing refugees to the state of Idaho, and particularly local governments, there should be some data that shows what we’re doing,” Anthon said. Though refugee data collection was the new element of the legislation, the focus remained on the impact on local law enforcement. Ada County Sheriff Matt Clifford said his primary concern with the legislation pertained to the requirement for nationality and immigration status to be determined throughout the “administration of criminal justice,” including detainment and other investigatory action taken by law enforcement that doesn’t necessarily lead to an arrest. ”I’m assuming we don’t want to be doing that on every traffic stop, just trying to figure out somebody’s nationality,” Clifford said. “That leads us down a bad road in litigation ... I certainly don’t want the (attorney general) investigating me for not enforcing the law.” Beyond the potential legal issues, Clifford said confusion could arise if new information is gathered from the time a person is detained to the time they are arrested and processed through a jail. At the time of detainment, a person could attest to being a lawful resident and a corresponding report would be submitted by the officer engaged in detainment. Once they are brought to a jail and have their fingerprints ran or their information reported to ICE databases, a jail deputy could come to the determination they were residing in the U.S. illegally, resulting in inconsistent reports for the same person, Clifford said. Clifford urged the committee to remove reporting for investigation and detainment as they often do not lead to arrest.
