entry.summary: The West Ada School District offices in Meridian. The West Ada School District’s board of trustees voted 4-1 Monday night, June 8, to terminate their contract with the Idaho School Boards Association (ISBA). Though the organization’s executive director, Misty Swanson, delivered a presentation arguing the benefits of continued participation and offered a discounted membership fee, all but Trustee Meghan Brown remained unconvinced. “I’ve no doubt that for the majority of districts in this state, you provide a tremendous value,” Trustee David Binetti told Swanson shortly before the vote. “But when it comes to services, as I go through the list of services [ISBA provides], we just either don’t need, don’t utilize or [already] effectively provide many of the services that would otherwise happen.” According to reporting from Idaho EdNews, West Ada will be the fourth public school district in the state not to affiliate with ISBA, with Kuna opting out of membership a month ago. The ISBA’s mission, according to its website, is to “Empower and champion school boards to foster student success.” Swanson cited board development workshops, complimentary access to trade publications, and nationwide superintendent search consultations well below the average rate as among the perks ISBA grants its members. She also offered to lower West Ada’s membership fee from the standard $45,000 to $35,000 for at least the coming year. “Our budget is just under $2 million,” Swanson said, in response to a trustee question about how West Ada exiting ISBA would impact the organization. “West Ada obviously contributes a lot to our membership dues.” Binetti questioned whether ISBA could adequately represent the district’s interests in a conflict where they would differ from those of another district, while trustee Evelyn McCullough and vice chair Rene Ozuna said that West Ada’s size meant it faced issues that other districts did not. West Ada is the largest school district in the Gem State, with nearly 38,000 pupils during the 2025-26 academic year. The Boise School District is second, with nearly 23,000 students. “West Ada has different needs given our size. We have legal counsel in-house,” Ozuna told Swanson. “Our policies don’t necessarily fit what you write for other districts.” Swanson proposed recruiting guest speakers from other states who represent similarly large districts to speak at ISBA events. Another issue raised was the lack of West Ada representation on ISBA’s government affairs committee. “I was testifying this year [in the state legislature], and the [ISBA] government affairs person stood up and testified to a bill that West Ada would never have supported,” Superintendent Derek Bub said. According to Bub, the last time West Ada drafted an ISBA resolution was several years ago, and the district no longer participated in ISBA’s insurance pool as it was intended for “smaller districts.” Though board members were impressed with ISBA’s rate of $9,750 to perform a nationwide superintendent search, it was not enough to convince most of them to vote to remain with ISBA. “We need to free up budget dollars,” Ozuna said. “We have other avenues for those services [ISBA provides].” During the same meeting, the trustees voted as part of their consent agenda to increase Bub’s salary by $20,000 annually for the next three years, raising it from $230,822 to $290,822. In a memo, the district said that their motivation for doing so was to keep salaries competitive with the national market. “The importance of this became increasingly clear during a recent national search for an executive position,” the June 8 memo said. “We found that many highly qualified candidates chose not to pursue opportunities with West Ada because compensation levels were not competitive with comparable school systems across the country.”

entry.title: University of Idaho Parma research center celebrates 100 — and 1 — years
entry.summary: Moscow Rep. Brandon Mitchell (left), Dean of the College of Agricultural and Life Sciences Leslie Edgar (center) and Lt. Gov. Scott Bedke (right) were each in attendance Friday for the University of Idaho’s centennial celebration of the Parma Research and Extension Center. Provided courtesy of the University of Idaho. Legislative members took a tour of the labs at the University of Idaho''s Parma research center during the facility''s centennial celebration Friday. From left to right: Associate Director of the Idaho Agricultural Experiment Station Dr. Juliet Marshall, Rep. Chris Bruce (R-Kuna), Sen. Brandon Shippy (R-New Plymouth) and Rep. Brandon Mitchell (R-Moscow). Photo provided courtesy of the University of Idaho. Parma R&E Center director Chris Caron delivered remarks outside the research facility Friday. The center celebrated 100 years of operation and stands at the cutting edge of agricultural research in the nation. Photo provided courtesy of the University of Idaho. PARMA — State leadership joined representatives of the University of Idaho on Friday to celebrate the centennial anniversary of the university’s Parma Research and Extension Center. The center was initially established in the early 20th century at the urging of local farmers. At the time, growers were dealing with alfalfa weevil, a type of beetle, and U of I entomologists were sent to help address the outbreak. Seeking to make this work in the region lasting, the agriculture industry in the area ended up funding the construction of a permanent entomology field station in Parma in 1925, which would become the research station it is known as today. Though keen readers will note the celebration was held a year after the center’s establishment, the observation being in 2026 was meant to align with the 125th anniversary of U of I’s College of Agriculture and Life Sciences. While the center today stands as a preeminent facility for cutting-edge research in the agricultural industry, Chris Caron, the Parma Research & Extension Center director, emphasized the facility predates many of the technological advancements we take for granted today. “I like to imagine the good folks of Parma at the grocery store in 1928 saying, ‘Wow, look at this sliced bread stuff, this is the best thing since they brought us Parma Research Station,’” Caron said. Today the center’s work is multi-faceted and far-reaching with a main station featuring 120 acres for crop and hop research, 80 acres dedicated to desert land, 30 acres for fruit research as well as laboratories that provide information ranging from the health of key pollinators to diseases that growers may be facing, the latter of which is facilitated through the National Plant Diagnostic Network (NPDN) hub lab. Ben Wood, a molecular diagnostician at the research center, said the resources available to his department are utilized by nearby residents, state, regional growers, commercial exporters who need their seeds examined in order to receive authorization to ship to other countries. “It’s a stressful job sometimes, when one day you’re just helping a person, a homeowner living nearby with a tree,” Wood said, “and then the next day we have a $3 million deal sitting in the port costing money every day.” If potential signs of pathogen-related issues arise, Wood said the center will interface directly with the impacted grower to recommend quarantining of crops or changes in growing methodologies to best determine the source of the issue. If a pathogen is found (such as blight in onions or potatoes), the center has to go through a variety of reporting layers to contain what could be an outbreak, as far as the Idaho State Department of Agriculture or the U.S. Department of Agriculture, Wood said. Despite the integral role the the center has played in advancing the agricultural sector from its inception, its future wasn’t always certain. At the height of the Great Recession in 2009, Leslie Edgar, the endowed dean of the College of Agricultural and Life Sciences, said the center was at one point slated for closure. Idaho Lt. Gov. Scott Bedke underscored that, given the return the center brings to Idaho’s agricultural industry, it should “never (be) on the chopping block again.” With investment in the center continuing today, Bedke said the center remains the envy of the state and the cutting edge research conducted in the area remains integral to the success of agriculture in Idaho both at home and abroad. The return on investment for the state can be seen through the nematology program, which plays a key role in diagnosing parasites in the region that bring issues in potatoes, sugar beets, onion, mint and other crops. According to the university, for every dollar spent on the nematodes program in “development, transfer and maintenance,” it brings a return of $52.17 to the state’s agriculture industry. As an example of the international ramifications the station’s work has for Idaho, Bedke recalled a trade mission he led in Asia last year. That trip included introductions to Idaho hops and a reinforcement of existing crop trades like white peaches, which are particularly popular in Taiwan, Bedke noted. While U of I’s research contributions help push crops outward, its prominence also brings added attention to the state as a leader in the field. “Everybody’s looking over the fence into Idaho, because we have very efficient farmers here, very efficient farming techniques, and they want to replicate that,” Bedke said. “Idaho stands for quality out there, and I think that all of that quality stuff starts in places like these extensions.” Emphasis on the cutting edge in the field — including efficiency and broad improvements to crop yields — are of particular concern for producers as the margins within the industry have tightened for an extended period. During a December agricultural summit hosted by Gov. Brad Little, agriculture leaders noted farm expenses have exceeded the prices received for most crops grown over the last five years. Given these conditions, “it’s even more important that we’re able to help producers understand what challenges may be facing their crop systems and how they can adjust to them,” Edgar said.

entry.title: Former school teacher arrested Thursday night on charges of sexual battery
entry.summary: NAMPA — A former Ridgevue High School teacher was arrested Thursday night and now faces decades in prison if convicted of the crimes with which he’s been accused. Nampa Police Special Enforcement Team arrested the former teacher “without incident,” according to a Nampa Police Department release, following a report of the former teacher’s suspicious behavior with a student. While listed on Ridgevue High School’s staff directory on Friday morning as a career and technical education faculty member, the district said Friday that he no longer works within the Vallivue School District. Troy Wayne McFadden, 46, of Nampa, faces two charges of felony sexual battery, according to online court records and the Canyon County Jail roster. The first charge is sexual battery of minor child 16 or 17 years of age-cause or have sexual contact with such minor child, not amounting to lewd conduct. The charge, if McFadden is convicted, could carry with it a sentence of up to 25 years in prison and a $50,000 fine. The second charge McFadden faces is sexual battery of a minor 16 or 17 years of age-solicit such minor child to participate in a sexual act. That charge also carries a maximum sentence of 25 years in prison and a $50,000 fine. McFadden, a retired U.S. Navy lieutenant commander, said few words at his video arraignment Friday afternoon from the Canyon County Jail, where he is currently booked on a $500,000 bond. McFadden’s attorney, Angela Chang, of Bartlett Law Defense Attorneys, tried to provide reasons for a $100,000 bond instead of a $500,000 bond. Those reasons included how he has no criminal history, no failures to appear, no substance abuse history and his status as a retired officer in the Navy “for over 25 years.” The state, however, argued the charges have an “extremely serious nature,” because McFadden, at least most recently, worked at a school. The state asked for $500,000 bond pre-trial release, GPS monitoring, and a no-contact order. Canyon County Magistrate Judge Chad Gulstrom noted there are “a number of aggravating circumstances given McFadden’s position in relation to the victim.” “At this point I am going to set bond in the amount of $500,000,” Gulstrom said. “I will require that he reports to pre-trial release and follow the standard reporting conditions.” McFadden also must abide by a no-contact order, including contact with the victim or any minor children, and is not go within 900 feet of the alleged victim, 900 feet from the victim’s residence, or 900 feet from Ridgevue High School. “This time there are no exceptions,” Gulstrom said before setting McFadden’s pre-trial hearing for June 26 at 8:30 a.m., with Canyon County Magistrate Judge Matthew Thompson. NPD detectives were made aware of a report of suspicious behavior involving McFadden on May 26, the report states. The incident involving McFadden, who was then a teacher at Ridgeview, allegedly included an interaction with a student off-campus, the release states. NPD detectives started an investigation at that point into the events surrounding the reported behavior. “Investigators believe this is an isolated incident and have no indication of other victims,” the release states. Parents are encouraged to speak with their children “who may have had contact with McFadden” and report any inappropriate conduct. NPD Sgt. Brad Barnum, of Nampa Police Persons Crimes Division, complimented the witness who came forward to report McFadden. “Witnessing suspicious behavior and alerting an adult is commendable,” Barnum said. “Those who notified police of something that appeared to be out of the norm and inappropriate were brave and did the right thing. With their help, we were able to stop a very dangerous situation. Protecting children in our community is one of our highest priorities at the Nampa Police Department. It does not matter what position the suspect holds; we are going to be relentless in seeking them out, investigating the allegations and holding them responsible.” On Friday morning, the Idaho Press reached out to the Vallivue School District seeking comment. The district replied Friday afternoon. “The Vallivue School District is aware of the arrest of a former teacher, as announced by the Nampa Police Department,” the statement read. “The individual is not currently employed by the Vallivue School District. Their teaching contract was not renewed for the upcoming school year.” The district, in its statement, referred to its own policy and Idaho Code 33-130 and 33-512(15), which states “all district employees undergo required criminal history and background checks as part of the employment process.” The district championed the steps the witness took to stop further interactions.

entry.title: Argument among Idaho elected officials breaks out over expenses at America250 meeting
entry.summary: Idaho Secretary of State Phil McGrane speaks to the America250 in Idaho Advisory Council on June 10, 2026, at the Idaho State Capitol in Boise. Originally published June 10 on IdahoCapitalSun.com. A late, unexpected request for additional money made by two of the warmup acts scheduled to perform at the upcoming America250 in Idaho Capitol Celebration in Boise played a role in setting off a contentious public argument between two statewide elected officials Wednesday at the Idaho State Capitol in Boise. For more than a year and a half, members of the America250 in Idaho Advisory Council have been planning local celebrations that are scheduled for July 4 to celebrate the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence being adopted in Philadelphia in 1776. With less than a month to go until the big events, the advisory council held one of its final meetings Wednesday to plan, coordinate and organize the America250 in Idaho celebrations, which are taking place in Boise and in local communities across the state. During Wednesday’s meeting, Idaho Secretary of State Phil McGrane, who is a member of the advisory council, made a motion to approve spending an additional $10,000 for unexpected expenses, including an additional $3,000 specifically requested by musicians Chad Marvin and EllieMae for the Boise celebration. McGrane also said there were additional unexpected budget considerations as well, including $1,600 for RV rentals to be used as “green rooms” for the performers and their equipment, $1,500 for Ada County Paramedics to staff the event and $6,000 to provide free water for the public. McGrane said the council has the funding available and the expenses were unexpected in nature, but came along with planning a large-scale event that he said could attract more than 20,000 people to the grounds surrounding the Idaho State Capitol. “We’re now at that point in it where we’re running up against the things, the unforeseen, and the issues that pop up (with planning large public events),” McGrane said. But Idaho State Treasurer Julie Ellsworth, who is also a member of the advisory council, said she has been “getting a lot of heat” for the amount of money the America250 in Idaho Advisory Council has been spending and balked at McGrane’s request. Ellsworth then publicly questioned McGrane’s motivations for the money. “There have been expenses that have been caused that have seen no fruition, that I believe have not really brought forth anything for this America250 in Idaho (celebration) in contracts and expenses — a lot of them you’ve been involved in, and ironically they equal about $10,000. And I’m just wondering if this is just to offset some of the work that was never approved but done, and somebody demanded money for?” Ellsworth asked McGrane during the meeting. McGrane responded immediately, and then offered a line-item breakdown of where the $10,000 would go toward, noting that any unused funding would be returned to the committee. “First of all, I find it offensive that you would suggest that,” McGrane told Ellsworth. “I think all of the work that was done was specifically for your office, and so … I’m not willing to engage unless you have anything specific that you want to infer or imply.” “I think there’s been extensive funding for some of your programs, and I don’t see this as a healthy debate,” McGrane added. “My focus, and the team in our office’s focus, is to make sure Idaho gets to celebrate America 250. This funding is specific to this, and I think the inference that there’s a blank check is offensive.” State officials said the advisory council has $54,967.53 in available funds to work with — enough to cover the $10,000. Marvin co-wrote and recorded “Esto Perpetua” an America250 in Idaho song that was already financed by the America250 in Idaho Advisory Council — at Ellsworth’s recommendation. Marvin and EllieMae have been announced as artists who will perform before headliner Josh Ritter during the America250 in Idaho Capitol Celebration, which is scheduled to take place July 4 at Cecil D. Andrus Park in front of the Idaho State Capitol. In addition to Ritter, other performers scheduled to perform at the America250 in Idaho Capitol Celebration include the Afrosonics, as well as Jeff Crosby and Willy Braun, according to members of the advisory council. However, members of the advisory council said Wednesday that while the announced performers have agreed to perform July 4, contracts with the musicians have not yet been signed. Committee members said that the local company Duck Club Entertainment, which created the annual Treefort Music Festival in Boise, is finalizing the contracts with the performers. The argument between Ellsworth and McGrane broke out a few moments after McGrane told the advisory council that Idaho was trying to avoid some of the issues with cancelled performances that have hampered separate, national America250 celebrations in recent weeks. “I don’t know if you’ve watched any of the news about the national event, but performers have been a thing on the national stage, and we’re doing everything we can to make it not a thing in Idaho, and we’ve got great performers,” McGrane said.

entry.title: Idaho Senate rejects 'tool of weaponization' bill granting AG greater power
entry.summary: BOISE — Ten days remain in the countdown to vote on Boise St… BOISE — Firefighting agencies gathered Tuesday at the Idaho … BOISE — The state Senate resoundingly rejected legislation Thursday that would have greatly expanded the powers of the Idaho Attorney General to pursue legal action to remove public officials and employees across state and local government. House Bill 896 was initially brought forward by House Speaker Mike Moyle and allows the attorney general to serve as a legal backstop in case a public official, public employee or government entity violates law without enforcement mechanisms. This situation arose when Boise Mayor McLean and Idaho Attorney General Raúl Labrador exchanged public letters over the city continuing to fly the LGBTQ+ Pride flag after being prohibited under a 2025 law that lacked an enforcement mechanism. Though the lack of enforcement was highlighted by McLean at the time, the omission was addressed in this year’s flag law. In the event similar situations were to arise in the future, House Bill 896 stipulated that any instance where a public official were to act in contradiction to Idaho prohibitions, the president pro tempore of the Senate, the speaker of the House of Representatives and the chair of a board of county commissioners can make referrals to the attorney general’s office regarding potential prohibition violations. If the attorney general believes a violation is occurring, they are required to provide the potentially violating party with 14 days notice to state intent to correct a violation or deny the violation was taking place. If the violation is not corrected, the attorney general can take the issue to court to for a decision on whether truly unlawful action is taking place. In addition to turning to the court to seek compliance, the attorney general would have also been allowed to seek to disqualify individuals from public office or public employment if proving someone “willfully” violated state prohibitions. If this were the case, the court would have been able to bar someone from seeking public office or employment in the state for up to five years. In an 8-27 vote, the Senate placed a definitive stamp of disapproval for this course of action being made available. Outside of Sen. Todd Lakey, R-Nampa, who served as the floor sponsor for the bill, debate from legislators from across the political spectrum was entirely negative. Members of both parties raised concerns the legislation would consolidate power in the state, lead to paranoia amongst public officials and employees and present a fundamentally unnecessary solution in the face of existing oversight mechanisms. Sen. Treg Bernt, R-Meridian, said the bill would allow the state to “supersede local decision making” by “stripping communities” of their ability to manage local issues. In addition to intruding on the relationship between state and local government, Bernt said the bill would bring “unprecedented authority” and investigatory power into the attorney general’s office. “This consolidation places critical decisions affecting freedom and democracy in the hands of a selected few, rather than dispersing power as intended in a representative government,” Bernt said. “This ship fundamentally alters the balance of power in the state of Idaho — I have grave concerns over this.” Sen. Ali Rabe, D-Boise, questioned why the legislation was necessary with the safeguards already in place. Even in bills that lack enforcement mechanisms, Rabe underlined there are mechanisms in place to “keep our elected officials” in check, including the Idaho Bribery and Corrupt Influences Act and the Idaho Ethics in Government Act, both of which govern the conduct of public officials. “I worry also it could put us at odds with each other and make us kind of paranoid against each other,” Rabe said. “It seems pretty easy in here to just send a referral from one elected official to another about anyone over anything. … I believe that it could potentially be abused in a lot of ways.” Pointing to the provision of the legislation that could bar people from public office for up to five years, Sen. Josh Kohl, R-Twin Falls, said blocking individuals from seeking office puts the state on “thin ice” in maintaining constitutional protections. “It’s just a tool of potential weaponization, and people could use this to target people they just don’t like or are feuding with. … You could imagine 1,000 scenarios where people could use this to target someone,” Sen. Brian Lenney, R-Nampa, said. Royce McCandless is the Statehouse reporter and covers Idaho politics. You can email him at . Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. Sign up now! To view our latest e-Edition, click the image on the left. Search the complete digital archives for all papers in the Pioneer News Group.

entry.title: Caldwell mayor attempts to veto resolutions, but has no legal authority
entry.summary: BOISE — Ten days remain in the countdown to vote on Boise St… BOISE — Firefighting agencies gathered Tuesday at the Idaho … A car crosses the railroad tracks at North 5th Avenue through Caldwell earlier this year. Mayor Eric Phillips on Wednesday, April 1, issued a veto of the quiet zone ordinance but the city attorney said in a legal opinion Phillips does not have the legal authority to do that. A train approaches the North 10th Avenue overpass as it passes through downtown Caldwell, Monday, Feb. 9. The railroad quiet zone saga has reemerged in Caldwell following Mayor Eric Phillips’ failed attempt to issue two vetoes on Wednesday, which have no legal effect and binding authority, according to a legal opinion by the city of Caldwell’s attorney. The city was set to begin the process of implementing a railroad quiet zone at seven of its crossings in central Caldwell following the 5-1 vote on two resolutions at a special city council meeting on March 25, which were to accept a bid proposal for the railroad quiet zone construction project — which would install security features at designated railroad crossings — and to approve and authorize the implementation of a railroad quiet zone. But Phillips — who was sworn in to lead the city in January — tried shooting down the council’s votes citing city code. In a legal opinion requested by Council Member Scott Tilmant and sent to the Idaho Press by Council Member Mike Dittenber, the city’s attorney, Oscar Klaas, referenced both city and Idaho code, which only allows a mayor to veto ordinances, not resolutions — therefore not giving Phillips legal authority to veto the quiet zone resolutions which were passed by the council. “... it is the legal opinion of the City Attorney that the veto authority of the mayor is limited to ordinances and does not extend to resolutions of the City Council. Therefore, the “vetoes” issued by the Mayor on April 1, 2026, are of no legal effect and have no binding authority,” Klaas said in his opinion. In a drawn-out statement through a city-issued press release, Phillips laid out his reasonings for his two attempted vetoes, pointing to ongoing public safety and fiscal concerns, and a lack of immediate necessity compared to other safety priorities needed throughout the city. “My responsibility as mayor is to ensure that decisions made by the city prioritize public safety, fiscal responsibility, and the long-term well-being of our community. At this time, I do not believe these actions meet that standard,” Phillips stated in a closing statement regarding Resolution No. 095-26 on the acceptance of a bid proposal. While an actual veto to a law by a mayor could be overridden by council at the next council meeting based on Idaho law, which in this instance would be done on Monday, April 6, Caldwell council members are not required to address or consider passing the quiet zone resolutions over the vetoes issued by the mayor, unless the council elects to do so, according to Klaas. “Any ordinance vetoed by the mayor may be passed over his veto by a vote of one-half (1/2) plus one (1) of the members of the full council,” according to state law. A designated quiet zone area does not require train engineers to routinely sound horns but still allows them to in the case of an emergency, according to federal regulations. Cities must install approved safety precautions by a diagnostic team — including representatives from the Federal Railroad Administration and Union Pacific — before a quiet zone can be implemented. There are over 1,000 existing quiet zones across the country that cover over 5,500 crossings. The decision to implement a railroad quiet zone at seven of Caldwell’s crossings, which are Fifth Avenue, Kimball Avenue, Ninth Avenue, 12th Avenue, 21st Avenue, Linden Street and Ustick Road, has been an ongoing topic that has caused heated debates over the last few months. And while the city has considered adding a quiet zone to its daily 17 trains for over a decade, the concept made progress last summer when the council approved the allocation of $400,000 in the FY 2026 budget to be used to establish a quiet zone. Project costs currently sit under budget at $238,000. Bruce Mills, the city’s deputy public works director of transportation, has researched the project for the last two years. Council members had a longer-than-intended wait to cast their votes for the quiet zone agreement and construction bid for the added safety features which were going to include a raised median curb, warning sign installations, and closing of access points close to the tracks. Phillips moved the items as old business in hopes to have further discussion with the council, as he made it clear that he was not in agreement with the timing of a quiet zone and raised concerns regarding pedestrian safety. But the council’s patience was cut short when Tilmant, a first-year council member, made a motion at the March 16 city council meeting to move the vote to the soonest upcoming council meeting. Dittenber said he feels disheartened by the divisiveness and questioned when the quiet zone saga will come to an end. “What I would hope was that this divisiveness, not only amongst council but also amongst the community, will come to an end where we can collectively work together to do great things for the great city of Caldwell,” Dittenber said. Dittenber believes that if there was a vote on a quiet zone again, it would still pass with a 5-1 vote to override the mayor’s veto. Currently, the mayor''s vetoes on the quiet zone resolutions are listed under old business for Monday’s city council meeting, but it is unclear if the council will choose to override them or not.

entry.title: Bill requiring local law enforcement to apply for agreements with ICE halted in the Senate
entry.summary: A bill that would have required law enforcement agencies in Idaho to apply for cooperative agreements with U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement has been halted in the Senate. BOISE — The Idaho Senate has halted the advancement of a bill that would have required law enforcement agencies to apply for cooperative agreements with U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement. Following the bill’s passage out of the House on Thursday, Sen. Mark Harris, R-Soda Springs, motioned to not concur with the House’s amendments to the legislation, preventing it from advancing on grounds that it violates Senate Rule 28(D). Under this rule, a bill cannot be amended to remove or incorporate provisions of another bill already pending in the Senate. Senate Bill 1247 was originally the Idaho e-verify act to require all public agencies to implement the federal e-verify program, which confirms the eligibility to work in the United States for new employees. The House amendment retooled the bill entirely to effectively swap a pending ICE cooperation bill with the stalled e-verify legislation. By unanimous consent, the Senate opted to not advance the reworked bill. Under the amended Senate bill, city and county law enforcement would be required to apply for cooperation agreements with U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement. These arrangements — known as section 287(g) memorandums of agreement — allow local law enforcement officers to be trained and deputized under programs that permit limited enforcement of federal immigration law. These programs function according to three separate models. A jail enforcement model allows officers to identify and process removable undocumented immigrants with either pending or active criminal charges. A task force model allows officers to have limited immigration enforcement authority in the performance of their routine police duties and participate in ICE-led task forces. A warrant service officer model allows officers to serve executive administrative warrants on undocumented immigrants in custody. Under each of these models, individual officers would need to participate in training and/or receive certification from ICE in order to be deputized to enforce federal immigration law. In cases where a law enforcement agency is “unable” to enter into a section 287(g) agreement, the law enforcement agency is required to publish a statement on the reasons for its inability to enter into an agreement as well as the alternative efforts made to cooperate with “enforcement and removal operation of immigration and customs enforcement.” According to the ICE website, a variety of sheriffs offices such as Kootenai and Owyhee counties are already engaged in some capacity with at least one of these three agreements. At the direction of Gov. Brad Little, the Idaho State Police also entered a 287(g) agreement last year to transport unauthorized immigrants convicted of crimes to deportation centers. This bill is a new version of House Bill 659, which passed out of the House last month before being held in Senate committee in a one-vote margin. In the public hearing before the House and the Senate, the measure was opposed by the police chiefs and county sheriffs who testified. Canyon County Sheriff Kieran Donahue, who testified on the previous bill on behalf of the Idaho Sheriffs Association, said sheriffs in the state were "absolutely opposed" to the legislation due to it mandating what sheriffs can and can''t do. He highlighted law enforcement would be on the hook for backfill and other expenses associated with officers departing for ICE-led training and would result in fewer officers to participate in daily patrol duties. In addition to testifying on previous renditions of the bill, Ada County Sheriff Matt Clifford issued a statement last week underscoring his office already cooperates with ICE on a daily basis to share arrest lists and comply with lawful requests to hold individuals for deportation. Clifford also emphasized that he has been able to align or decouple his agency depending on when federal objectives align with the needs of the county. "In my role as sheriff, I have made decisions to end partnerships with federal agencies when their demands or direction were overreaching and no longer in the best interest of Ada County," Clifford stated. "A forced relationship with ICE under a mandated program gives me no such option when participation begins to erode what is best for our community." During debate on the House floor, Rep. Britt Raybould, R-Rexburg, questioned why the legislature was seeking to force law enforcement’s hand in the matter when agencies are already sharing relevant information with ICE or participating in 287(g) agreements themselves. Local law enforcement can already enter 287(g) agreements if they feel doing so is in the best interest of their department and community, whereas this legislation would make applying for these agreements no longer subject to agency discretion, Raybould said. “The idea that we would allow and encourage the federal overriding of the discretion of our local law enforcement does not sit well with me,” Raybould said.

entry.title: Legislature passes bill making ACHD races partisan and countywide starting in 2028
entry.summary: This photo provided by the Ada County Highway District shows a road managed by the county agency. Starting in 2028, ACHD elections will be partisan and countywide, instead of by district. BOISE — The Idaho Legislature approved a bill Wednesday that would make Ada County Highway District elections partisan and countywide. Similar changes to the body were considered last session before they died in committee. The bill was fast-tracked through the legislature Wednesday, passing out of the Senate in the afternoon and sent to the House for a hearing later that evening. Late Wednesday night, the House passed the legislation in a 47-20 vote. The bill is now poised to cap off a multi-year effort to transform Idaho’s only countywide highway district into a partisan body. Though ACHD would keep its current five sub-district arrangement, commissioners would no longer be elected solely by the residents of their respective district and instead be required to participate in partisan elections with associated primaries after operating as a non-partisan body since its inception. Once signed into law, the changes would go into effect for the 2028 election cycle. In her presentation of the legislation on the Senate floor, Sen. Lori Den Hartog, R-Meridian, said the changes would make ACHD elections mirror those of the Ada County Commissioners, who are required to live in a certain district but are elected by every resident of the county. Den Hartog said the shift to countywide elections would allow for the voices of individuals who travel across the county to be heard by commissioners representing the different sub-districts. With several state highways running through the county, Den Hartog said it was important to consider the regional needs of the county as ACHD projects frequently bump up against the highways under the Idaho Transportation Department’s jurisdiction. “As you think about how that state system interacts with the local system here, it’s really important to consider all of those regional needs and make sure that the voice of everyone in Ada County is heard at the ballot box,” Den Hartog said. Alexis Pickering, vice president of the ACHD commissioner board, testified against the legislation during the Wednesday hearing in the House. For commissioners on the body, the five districts of approximately 100,000 are already double to size of legislative districts in Idaho. Shifting the voter base to Ada County’s population, which exceeds 500,000, would make ACHD commissioners beholden to a population the size of a congressional district while fulfilling part-time positions, Pickering said. Miranda Gold, ACHD commission president, testified on behalf of the commission and similarly spoke against the legislation she perceived as marking a sea change for how the commission operates. When the body was formed with the approval of Ada County voters in the 1970s, Gold said the thinking at the time was countywide elections with sub-district residency requirements would “dilute representation” in parts of the county and change the “tone and tenor of the commission.” ”A candidate living in Kuna would need to campaign in Star, and a commissioner could be elected by 80% of voters who do not live in their district,” Gold said. In introducing partisanship and districtwide elections to the body, commissioners will be turned into “full-time fundraisers,” introducing moneyed interests to these races and politicizing transportation projects, Gold said. Echoing Den Hartog, Rep. Jason Monks, R-Meridian, maintained on the House floor that the changes were simply to mirror the arrangement for the county commissioners. ”We’re trying to say that ACHD operates very similar to your county commissioners,” Monks said, “and they should be treated the same.” Rep. Steve Berch, D-Boise, testified twice against the legislation on the House floor and said rejecting the legislation was a matter of remaining consistent for the legislature. He noted in the past sessions the legislature enacted requirements that city council members in cities of 100,000 or more be elected by and live in particular districts. ”If we want to be consistent, we should be requiring the Ada County Commissioners be elected by the district that they’re in, instead of districtwide,” Berch said. Royce McCandless is the Statehouse reporter and covers Idaho politics. You can email him at .

entry.title: JFAC votes to withhold more than $50 million from general fund, opting for larger bottom line
entry.summary: Legislators at work on the floor of the Idaho Senate as the end of the legislative session draws near Tuesday, March 31, 2026. BOISE — Throughout the legislative session, leadership on the Joint Finance-Appropriations Committee has framed 2% cuts to state agencies for the next fiscal year as a necessary action to ensure the state’s budget was in a “structurally sound” position. On Wednesday, however, the committee voted to have tens of million in funds held in the state’s Budget Stabilization Fund that would have otherwise been automatically transferred to bolster the coming year’s bottom line. Existing code outlining the function of the Budget Stabilization Fund, also known as Idaho’s rainy day fund, states the balance shall not exceed 15% of the total general fund for the ending fiscal year. For fiscal year 2027, this amount would be based upon the fiscal year 2026 revenue, which JFAC projected at $5.51 billion. If exceeding this 15% threshold, the state controller is directed to transfer this money to the general fund unless the legislature acts to reduce the amount of the transfer, which JFAC initiated on Wednesday. This stabilization fund balance currently sits at $880.2 million. When compared to a projected general fund revenue of $5.51 billion for fiscal year 2026, this amounts to a difference of $53.7 million beyond the 15% cap. “It makes no sense to me that we would set aside code and not put this money in the general fund, especially when we have cut budgets to the bone and then some,” Sen. Janie Ward-Engelking, D-Boise, said. This amount of budget stabilization funds that would have otherwise been moved automatically is well above the $30.1 million in ongoing 2% cuts JFAC initially approved for most state agencies in fiscal year 2027. Though several restorations, including those for wildfire suppression, were fully or partially enacted by the body in the time since, this was not true for every agency. In the case of Idaho’s colleges and universities, JFAC voted against restoring a 2% ongoing cut, or $7.3 million general fund reduction for these institutions. “We should have been budgeting for this on the green sheet from the start of session,” Keith Bybee, budget and policy division manager of the Legislative Services Office said in reference to the daily budget update form issued by LSO. “… With the revenue number that the legislature adopted, that would have triggered an automatic transfer from the budget stabilization fund in 2027 back to the general fund.” Bybee said this has not been an issue until the last two sessions when the legislature saw hundreds of millions of dollars in budget surpluses that resulted in this stabilization fund hitting the 15% cap. These were both years that did not see broad agency cuts to state agencies, as was the case this legislative session. Sen. Melissa Wintrow, D-Boise, questioned why the body wouldn’t want the excess funds automatically transferring to the general fund, given the budget constraints of this session. Bybee responded it was a policy decision by the body to leave excess funds on the bottom line for the coming fiscal year. Last week JFAC advanced a series of cash transfers that will only take place if the state’s general fund balance falls below $150 million. JFAC co-chairs Sen. Scott Grow, R-Eagle, and Rep. Josh Tanner, R-Eagle, said the intent is to ensure the state has a “cushion” heading into next year. Sen. Jim Woodward, R-Sagle, took issue with the committee’s course of action and said the 15% cap on the budget stabilization fund was in place to ensure current taxpayers have their dollars appropriately utilized in the present. ”We are asking today’s taxpayers to take care of tomorrow’s taxpayers’ problems,” Woodward said. “The people who are putting money in this budget stabilization fund today may not be Idahoans when it gets spent.” Despite reservations voiced by several committee members on not allowing for the automatic transfer to take place, JFAC voted 16-4 to hold the excess budget stabilization funds for the next fiscal year. Ward-Engelking, Wintrow, Rep. Brooke Green, D-Boise, and Rep. Rod Furniss, R-Rigby, each voted against. Royce McCandless is the Statehouse reporter and covers Idaho politics. You can email him at . Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. Sign up now! To view our latest e-Edition, click the image on the left. Search the complete digital archives for all papers in the Pioneer News Group.

entry.title: A tearful Boise City Council declares Trans Day of Visibility
entry.summary: BOISE — Ten days remain in the countdown to vote on Boise St… BOISE — After a yearlong search, Boise State University has … Boise Mayor Lauren McLean responds to the crowd at the Boise Pride parade in 2023. A heaviness set the tone of Tuesday’s Boise City Council meeting, on the heels of anti-LGBTQ+ legislation being signed into Idaho law, as Boise declared March 31 as the Trans Day of Visibility. Council members spoke through tears at the special meeting as they reflected on the importance of allyship following the passage of a flag ban earlier that day. After over a decade of the Pride flag flying outside Boise City Hall, Gov. Brad Little signed an amended law to restrict what flags cities and counties can display. The bill, HB 561, comes after previous attempts to outlaw the Pride flag on city and county property in the 2025 legislative session. Skirting around the ban, Mayor Lauren McLean and the city council continued flying the flag due to a lack of enforcement mechanisms in the 2025 legislation. The updated legislation will now enforce a $2,000 fine for each day an unauthorized flag is displayed. Acceptable flags include the U.S. flag, state flags, military flags, the POW/MIA flag, flags of Indian tribes, and country flags for special occasions. On Tuesday, McLean spoke to a packed room. Over the past year, she has stood firmly in the city’s right to show support for the LGBTQ+ community. “I hope that knowing that you are loved, seen and welcomed by this city enables you to participate in the fabric of this community,” McLean said. She described the day as a sad one for the people who call Boise home, but welcomed the Trans Day of Visibility proclamation as a way to show support for a marginalized population. Tuesday also saw the signing of HB 752, a bill that criminalizes the use of public facilities that do not align with a person’s “biological sex.” A repeat offender will be subject to a felony charge. “They are seeking to divide us by targeting the most vulnerable among us,” McLean said about lawmakers. “There’s little we can do at the state and national level, but there is much that can be done here at home.” McLean choked up as she discussed the future of Idaho’s next generation, describing mothers who have to watch their children leave the state. She seemingly referred to the strained state of Idaho’s obstetrics care and abortion rights. Idaho has some of the most restrictive abortion laws in the nation. With the overturning of Roe v. Wade by the U.S. Supreme Court in 2022, an abortion trigger ban quickly went into effect. “I thought about my own kid, and their questions of whether or not their friends are welcome here,” McLean said. “Whether as a woman, my daughter will be able to have children here in a healthy and safe way.” Council Member Colin Nash reflected on his previous experience as a representative for District 16 in the Idaho House. He resigned in 2024, citing his intent to focus more on city council. “Often in the legislature, it felt like casting your pearls before swine,” Nash said. Nash spoke about his wife’s cousin, Shay, who killed herself a few years ago after a lack of acceptance for identifying with the LGBTQ+ community. As a representative at the time Nash wanted to share her story in the legislature, but, as he expected from previous experience, “it just didn’t resonate, their hearts were hardened.” “Shay was a lovely soul,” Nash said, speaking through tears. “... I’m not asking you to empathize, but if someone feels something so deeply that they would go to those ends, can we just give them a little bit more understanding? A little bit more sympathy?” Sporting a rainbow sticker on her blazer, Council Pro Tem Kathy Corless spoke on the importance of visibility. While she identifies herself as a longtime LGBTQ+ ally, Corless said she has realized a need to be more transparent.

entry.title: JFAC takes lead on Rural Health Transformation Program after previous bills stalled
entry.summary: The door to the Joint Finance Appropriations Committee (JFAC) meeting room at the Idaho State Capitol, Wednesday, March 4. BOISE — With past efforts to provide direction on the Rural Health Transformation Program floundering this session, the Joint Finance-Appropriations Committee took it upon themselves Tuesday to advance guardrails for how hundreds of millions in health care grants are to be spent. The Rural Health Transformation Program (RHTP) was established last year following the passage of the One Big Beautiful Bill. The $50 billion in funding for the program is scheduled to be distributed to all 50 states in $10 billion increments from 2026 through 2030. Idaho was awarded $929 million over this time frame in increments of $186 million that will each be available for two years before expiring. A prior survey issued by the Department of Health and Welfare identified the top three uses for Idaho’s funds as being recruiting and retaining clinical workforce talent; assisting rural communities in identifying their health care needs across the system including emergency, pre-hospital and outpatient care; and improving prevention and chronic disease management. To prepare for the allocation of this funding, JFAC advanced legislation to establish the Idaho rural health transformation fund comprised of these federal funds that are to be distributed according to recommendations from a new rural health transformation committee. Though several bills to provide oversight on this funding have been presented by legislators this session, the key sticking point has been the makeup of this committee, which has grown from five members to nine members across a series of bills originating in both the House and the Senate. The legislation approved by JFAC states the committee will consist of nine members with four coming from the Senate and appointed by the Senate President Pro Tempore (currently Sen. Kelly Anthon, R-Rupert), four members coming from the House and appointed by the Speaker of the House (currently Rep. Mike Moyle, R-Star) and one non-voting member elected by the governor. Alex Williamson, senior analyst for the Legislative Services Office, said the committee makeup mirrors what House Bill 916 intended to establish. Though that bill unanimously passed the House earlier this month, it did not receive a hearing from the Senate Health and Welfare Committee. The Senate instead voted to support Senate Bill 1264, which included specifications for the nine-member committee to include three representatives and three senators representing rural districts, defined as those representing districts with a population center of no more than 20,000 people. Though not including this rural representation stipulation, the legislation from JFAC includes a provision from the Senate bill requiring committee members have access to data on RHTP including awards received or created by the Idaho Department of Health and Welfare. Sen. Kevin Cook, R-Idaho Falls, who was the floor sponsor for Senate Bill 1264, questioned why the Senate bill was not set to have a hearing in the House after passing the Senate on Monday. Rep. Josh Tanner, R-Eagle, who co-chairs JFAC, said House leadership stated the bill was not going to be heard and the bill before JFAC aligned with their recommendations. Cook responded that the removal of the requirement for rural representation on the committee would result in legislators representing areas serviced by urban health care giants St. Luke’s and Saint Alphonsus making funding decisions for rural hospitals, rural health care and rural constituents. “I think that is just really, really wrong,” Cook said, “… why would you want city people representing rural?” After Cook voiced these concerns, JFAC unanimously approved the legislation establishing the nine-member oversight committee without rural district requirements, which will go before both the House and the Senate for a wider floor vote. In separate funding consideration for the first and second years of the RHTP, JFAC aligned with recommendations from Gov. Brad Little to allocate resources to 12 “limited service” full-time positions — which will be in place for the duration of the federal program — along with $3.7 million in federal funds for fiscal year 2026 for a portion of this year’s award. In a 14-4 decision the committee voted to align with the governor and allocate these resources for the current fiscal year. A subsequent request for $295.4 million — comprised of funds for the first and second year of federal awards and the aforementioned 12 employees — was approved for fiscal year 2027 in a 15-4 vote. Williamson with the Legislative Services Office said the supplemental funding allows the department to begin moving on the federal program and start appropriating the funds for different rural health objectives. “The state does have an obligation to obligate those funds for year one by October of this year,” Williamson said. “So allowing a supplemental would give them a bit longer of a runway on getting those dollars out the door.” Royce McCandless is the Statehouse reporter and covers Idaho politics. You can email him at . Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. Sign up now!

entry.title: City of Boise takes down Pride flag after Gov. Little signs flag restrictions into law
entry.summary: BOISE — Ten days remain in the countdown to vote on Boise St… BOISE — Firefighting agencies gathered Tuesday at the Idaho … Flags fly outside Bose City Hall, including the Pride flag and flag honoring organ and tissue donors, in April 2025. The Pride flag was taken down Tuesday by Mayor Lauren McLean to comply with a new state law that restricts what flags can be flown on city and county property. BOISE — After more than a decade of flying the Pride flag outside city hall, Boise Mayor Lauren McLean ordered the LGBTQ+ Pride flag be taken down to comply with a new law placing significant restrictions on what flags can be flown on city and county property and imposing steep fines for violations. Gov. Brad Little signed into law HB 561 on Tuesday, restricting what flags cities and counties are able to fly as part of an effort to end the flying of the LGBTQ+ Pride flag in Boise. The law went into effect immediately after its signing. “This law seeks to erase both the values we hold dear and people who are integral to the fabric of our community,” McLean said in a news release from the city of Boise. “We will continue to celebrate the vibrancy of our community, the diversity of our residents, and our North Star of being a safe and welcoming city for everyone.” The legislation authored by Rep. Ted Hill, R-Eagle, restricts cities and counties to flying the United States flag, official flags of any state in the United States, official flags of military branches and units, the POW/MIA flag, the official flag of recognized Indian tribes and the official flags of countries other than the United States for special occasions, with exceptions for nations the U.S. is engaged in hostile action against. The legislation was amended several times this legislative session to allow for carve-outs for the flying of the Canadian flag in Bonners Ferry and the flying of the Basque autonomous community flag after legislators and members of the public took issue with these flags potentially being unable to fly on city- and county-owned property after several decades of doing so. McLean stated her decision to take down the flag on Tuesday was the result of the legislation’s substantial financial penalty, which would require the city to pay $2,000 per violating flag for each day they are displayed. Before a civil fine can be imposed, violating entities are notified by the attorney general, who provides 10 days to correct the violation before civil action is taken. If not corrected in this time frame — or if the entity has had similar violations in the past — the attorney general can take civil action immediately. This enforcement provision was notably absent from the state’s previous flag law and led to a public exchange of letters between McLean and Idaho Attorney General Raúl Labrador after the city continued to fly the Pride flag along with a flag honoring organ and tissue donors. After this dispute, McLean — with support from city council — designated the Pride flag and the organ donor flag as “official” flags of the city of Boise to comply with the previous law. This option will no longer be available to Boise or any city moving forward as cities and counties can only use flags established as “official” prior to Jan. 1, 2023. As for the reasoning behind this date, Hill previously said city flags have become a “political enterprise” in the time since. McLean stated in the release the city was reviewing “all legal avenues and considering next steps” in light of the new law being enacted and had a “responsibility to respond with both conviction and care.” ”To our LGBTQ+ friends, family, and neighbors: you are an essential part of Boise,” McLean stated. “You are welcome here. You belong here. And no law can change that.” Editor''s Note: This article has been corrected to accurately identify the bill number of the new flag legislation. It is House Bill 561. Royce McCandless is the Statehouse reporter and covers Idaho politics. You can email him at . Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. Sign up now! To view our latest e-Edition, click the image on the left. Search the complete digital archives for all papers in the Pioneer News Group.

entry.title: Committee sends ICE agreement bill for amendment
entry.summary: A bill that would have required law enforcement agencies in Idaho to apply for cooperative agreements with U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement has been halted in the Senate. BOISE — The Senate State Affairs Committee opted to differ with law enforcement in sending one immigration enforcement bill for amendment, but they aligned with law enforcement’s concerns by rejecting another. The immigration enforcement bill advanced to the amending order by the committee on Monday is a renewed effort to require local law enforcement — including police departments and sheriffs’ offices — to apply for cooperation programs with U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement. These programs, known as 287(g) agreements, allow local law enforcement to be deputized as immigration enforcement agents to enforce federal law. House Bill 659 previously died in committee after receiving negative testimony from local law enforcement who argued the bill would result in officers being taken off patrols and restrict the ability for these agencies to make decisions that are in their best interest, particularly as federal objectives change significantly with time from one administration to the next. Sen. Kelly Anthon, R-Rupert, who sponsored the legislation, said his aim was to “bridge the gap” between the previous legislation and law enforcement concerns. The key change in the legislation pertains to the application process. Idaho law enforcement agencies would be required to apply for 287(g) agreements with ICE, even if such agreements can’t be fulfilled. However, under the bill, participation is only required “to the extent that the necessary resources for participation are reasonably available to the law enforcement agency.” If this is the case, county commissioners or city councils are directed to issue a finding of fact for why entering into such an agreement would hinder their law enforcement agency. Several of the law enforcement leaders who testified against the previous bill came before the Senate committee Monday voicing the same concerns from the previous bill. Canyon County Sheriff Kieran Donahue said the new bill fails to eliminate the mandate that law enforcement apply for these agreements, presenting a “real danger for the state of Idaho” in requiring law enforcement to enter into these agreements and in turn dictate how Idaho law enforcement is utilized. Ada County Sheriff Matt Clifford, speaking on behalf of the Idaho Sheriffs Association, said the association was opposed to the legislation on the grounds it would take away the ability for law enforcement agencies to make the best decisions for themselves. He referred to a prior Memorandum of Understanding, or cooperative agreement, with the U.S. Marshals under the Biden administration that would have required local law enforcement to give all evidence from a critical incident to the federal government including body camera footage, reports and evidence that would have prevented local law enforcement from investigating a shooting. Though new agreements with the U.S. Marshals have since been made under the Trump administration, Clifford said the ability to previously say “‘I’m not signing that,’” would no longer be permissible when it came to ICE. “This takes that away,” Clifford said. “There are back doors written into here for inabilities, not for, ‘this is not good for the community anymore.’” Sen. Ben Adams, R-Nampa, said his support for the legislation was instead born out of a desire to respond to Idahoans calling for more robust immigration enforcement in this state. “This approach — although not seen as the best move by all — is a way to ensure to the citizens of Idaho that their law enforcement is proactive in helping the federal government address that issue,” Adams said. “That’s what I see what this is about.” In a 6-3 vote, the committee voted to send Anthon’s bill for amendment prior to being debated on the Senate floor. Anthon said the key part of the bill he is looking to clarify is when the Federal Tort Claim Act applies to allow private parties to sue the U.S. government. Additional language would be needed to outline the Idaho officers are subject to the state’s own tort claim or liability legislation even when working on behalf of the federal government, Anthon said. Committee rejects conditional release to ICE Sen. Lori Den Hartog, R-Meridian introduced new legislation allowing individuals in the custody of the Idaho Department of Correction subject to a federal immigration detainer to be released to ICE prior the completion of their sentence from crimes committed. Under the bill, the IDOC director would be able to reach an agreement with ICE to move an offender to an ICE facility prior to the expiration of the offenders’ prison sentence, with exceptions for life sentences.
